questions.subject: Economy
questions.questionText:
Consider the following:
I. - Tractor used for ploughing the field
II. 	 Cattle used for dairy purposes
III. 	 Expenditure on pesticides
IV. 	 Installation of submersible pumps in borewells
V. - Packaging used for finished products
How many of the above are part of Gross Capital Formation (GCF) in agriculture?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Capital used in agriculture can broadly be classified into two categories: o **Fixed Capital** refers to that capital which lasts for more than one year. o **Working Capital** refers to that capital which lasts less than one year.
● Fixed Capital Formation can further be classified into **Gross Fixed Capital Formation (GFCF)** and **Net** **Fixed Capital Formation (NFCF).**
o The GFCF consists of the **sum of all additions to the existing stock of fixed capital** in the
current year while NFCF refers to **GFCF net of depreciation** in the current year. o **Depreciation** [i.e. consumption of fixed capital (CFC)] is calculated only for all fixed assets (tangible
and intangible).
● GFCF comprises investment made in machines, tools, farm building, **tractor**, land levellers, cultivators, harvesters, **dug well & tube well**, irrigation structure, tree-stock, livestock, land development, soil & water conservation harvesting structures, etc. These are assets created which would help raise the farm production, productivity and income.
● Hence, **tractors** **used for ploughing the field and installation of submersible pumps in borewells** are long-term productive assets and form a part of GFCF. **So, points I and IV are correct.**
● Animals that are used in production year after year are classified under GFCF, which include, **breeding** **stock, dairy cattle, sheep reared for wool and draught animals**. Animals raised for **slaughter,** **including poultry,** are not fixed assets. **So, point II is correct.**
● Expenses on **seeds, fertilizers, pesticides, wages to the workers**, etc. are considered as **working** **capital,** as they last for less than one year. **So, point III is not correct.**
● **Inventories,** which include materials and supplies meant for intermediate inputs in production and finished goods for sale **(e.g. packaging),** are part of **working capital**, since they are classified as current assets because they are typically consumed within a year as part of the production process. **So, point V is not** **correct.** **So, only three of the above** are part of Gross Capital Formation (GCF) in agriculture. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box:**
● **Capital Accumulation:** Refers to the stock of capital asset over a period when the rate of depreciation in the asset is lower than the rate of investment made in it.
● **Tangible Capital:** Refers to the productive physical assets such as tractor, farmhouses, tube-wells, diesel engines, etc.
● **Intangible Capital:** It consists of non-material assets that contribute to agricultural output such as investment in workers’ education, training and health, etc.
● **Capital Consumption:** It refers to that part of fixed capital which is consumed while producing output during the current year. It is also known as depreciation.
● **Institutional Credit:** It refers to the credit/loan provided by the financial institutions, such as commercial banks, cooperative banks and region rural banks.

questions.subject: Economy
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference to the remittances in India:
I. - They are recorded under the current account of the balance of payments.
II. 	 They are classified as part of official foreign exchange reserves.
III. 	 India receives its highest remittances from the United Kingdom (UK).
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Remittance refers to the act of **sending money back to a recipient**, particularly one residing in another country. Remittances primarily originate from **individuals employed overseas** who send financial support to their families in their home countries.
● Remittances are an increasingly important and relatively **stable source of external finance** that often play a critical role in the development of under-developed countries. Particularly as it appears to be **less** **volatile, pro-cyclical** and therefore a more reliable source of foreign currency than other capital flow like; direct foreign investment, development aid.
○ Thus, foreign exchange earnings through remittances **alleviate the foreign exchange crisis**, support the balance of payment problem by enabling imports of capital goods and raw materials for industrialization.
● The remittances are **current account inflows.** They represent money sent by individuals working abroad to their home countries, which is a type of current transfer. **So, statement I is correct.**
● Remittances are **not part of India's official foreign exchange reserves.** The reserves include foreign currencies, gold, Special Drawing Rights (SDRs), and reserve tranche positions with the IMF. **So,** **statement II is not correct.**
● India has emerged as one of the world s leading recipients of remittances, with inflows more than doubling from US$ 55.6 billion in 2010-11 to US$ 118.7 billion in 2023-24, according to the Reserve Bank of India’s (RBI) latest Remittances Survey 2025.
○ In FY24, over half of India’s remittances came from advanced economies, including the **United** **States of America**, United Kingdom, Singapore, Canada, and Australia, signalling a shift in the global remittance landscape.
■ The **US emerged as the largest source, accounting for 27.7 per cent of total**
**remittances in FY24**, up from 23.4 per cent in FY21. The United Arab Emirates (UAE) followed as the second-largest source, though its share fell to 19.2 per cent in FY24 from 27 per cent in FY17. **So, statement III is not correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box:**
● **Balance of Payments (BoP):** BoP is the **economic transactions of an** **economy** with the Rest of the World (i.e.transactions between resident & nonresident entities) during a given period. It comprises **current, capital and** **financial accounts**.
o It can be better appreciated in terms of the national income accounting
identity: **GDP = C+G+I+X-M.** o In other words, domestic output (GDP) is equal to private consumption(C),
plus government consumption (G), plus domestic investment (I), plus net exports (X-M). If net exports of goods and services (X-M) are negative (i.e. Balance of Trade is negative or there is a trade (including services) deficit,), the domestic economy is absorbing more than it can produce.
● **Current Account:** o Current account covers all transactions (other than those in financial items)
that involve economic values and occur between resident and non-resident entities. Also covered are offsets to current economic values provided or acquired without a quid pro quo. Specifically, the major classifications are goods and services, income, and current transfers.
● **Capital Account:** o The major components of the capital account are (a) capital transfers and
(b) acquisition/disposal of non-produced, nonfinancial assets.
● **Financial Account:** o The financial account records an economy’s transaction in external financial
assets and liabilities. All components are classified according to type of investment or by functional subdivision **((a) direct investment, (b)** **portfolio investment, (c) other investment, (d) reserve assets).** **Relevance**: According to a recent report by the RBI, developed economies emerged as the top source of inward remittances for Indians in the last four years.

questions.subject: Economy
questions.questionText:
With reference to the rupee Convertibility in India, consider the following statements:
I. - Current Account Convertibility allows to convert the rupees into other internationally accepted currencies.
II. 	 India currently has partial Capital Account Convertibility (CAC) for both inflows and outflows by residents and non-residents.
III. 	 The S.S. Tarapore Committees stressed fiscal consolidation as a precondition for capital account convertibility.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Answer: a** **Explanation**: **Currency convertibility** refers to the freedom to convert the domestic currency into other internationally accepted currencies and vice versa. **Current account convertibility** refers to freedom in respect of ‘payments and transfers for current international transactions’, while **Capital Account Convertibility (CAC)** refers to **freedom of currency conversion** in relation to capital transactions in terms of inflows and outflows.
● **Current Account Convertibility** refers to the degree of **freedom to convert the rupees into** **other internationally accepted currencies** and vice versa without any restrictions whenever payments are made. **So, statement I is correct.**
● **India currently has partial Capital Account Convertibility.** There are various restrictions and regulations on both capital inflows (limits on foreign direct investment in certain sectors) and outflows (limits on overseas direct investment by Indian companies, restrictions on individuals investing abroad beyond certain limits). **This "partial" convertibility allows** for managed capital flows to support economic growth while mitigating risks. **So, statement II is correct.**
o **India has current account convertibility for both inflows and outflows by residents**
**and non-residents**. The Indian Rupee was made partly convertible on current account from March 1992 under the “Liberalised Exchange Rate Management scheme, and fully convertible on current account in 1994, following the acceptance of the obligations under Article VIII of the IMF’s Articles of Agreement in August 1994.
● The **S.S Tarapore Committees (1997 and 2006)** on Capital Account Convertibility highlighted fiscal consolidation and reduction of the fiscal deficit as a **crucial precondition for moving towards fuller** **capital account convertibility**. **So, statement III is correct.**
o According to the committee, one of the most important pre-conditions for capital account
convertibility is strong and stable macroeconomic conditions and a sustainable fiscal deficit. Fiscal deficit as a percentage of GDP is desired to be around 3-3.5% of GDP. **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Economy
questions.questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
Organisation Motto
I. - Central Board of Indirect Taxes and Customs
A person who is awake in those that sleep
II. - Income Tax Department Treasury is the basis of Power
III. - Directorate of Enforcement Industry, Impartiality and Integrity
How many of the pairs given above are correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Central Board of Indirect Taxes and Customs** (erstwhile Central Board of Excise & Customs) is a part of the **Department of Revenue under the Ministry of Finance**, Government of India. It deals with the tasks of formulation of policy concerning levy and collection of Customs, Central Excise duties, Central Goods & Services Tax and IGST, prevention of smuggling and administration of matters relating to Customs, Central Excise, Central Goods & Services Tax, IGST and Narcotics to the extent under CBIC's purview. The Board is the administrative authority for its subordinate organizations, including Custom Houses, Central Excise and Central GST Commissionerates and the Central Revenues Control Laboratory.
○ "**Ya Aeshu Suptaeshu Jagruti," which translates to "a person who is awake in those** **that sleep"** is the official motto of **Institute of Chartered Accountants of India** (ICAI). This Sanskrit phrase is a quotation from the Upanishads (specifically the Katha Upanishad). **So,** **pair I is not correctly matched.**
● The **Income Tax Department** is a government agency undertaking direct tax collection of the Government of India. It functions under the Department of Revenue of the Ministry of Finance. Kautilya's dictum **“Kosha Moolo Danda (Treasury is the basis of Power)”** is the motto of the **Income Tax** **Department. So, pair II is correctly matched.**
● **The Directorate of Enforcement (ED)** is a multi-disciplinary organization mandated with investigation of the offence of money laundering and violations of foreign exchange laws. It does not have a formally declared motto.
○ **The motto "Industry, Impartiality and Integrity" is of the Central Bureau of** **Investigation (CBI). So, pair III is not correctly matched.** **So, only one of the pairs given above is correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.microTopic: Interior of Earth and Continental Theories
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference to the Fertilizer Subsidy regime in India:
I. - Subsidies for Di-Ammonium Phosphate (DAP) are provided based on its nutrient content directly to the farmers.
II.  	 Urea is subsidized at a fixed rate per kilogram.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The Government has been implementing the **Nutrient Based Subsidy (NBS) scheme**,  for **Phosphatic & Potassic (P&K) fertilizers**.
● Under the NBS scheme, **a** **fixed amount of subsidy**, decided on an annual/bi-annual basis, is provided on subsidized P&K fertilizers **depending on the** **actual nutrients contained in the fertilizers** **(Nitrogen, Phosphorus, Potassium and Sulphur)** to encourage the use of fertilizers specific to the nutrient requirements of the soil and crops.
○ Under the NBS scheme, the P&K sector is decontrolled, **fertilizer companies are allowed to** **fix MRP** at reasonable levels which is monitored by the Government. ○ The government provides subsidies **directly to fertilizer manufacturers and importers** **(and not to the farmers)** to maintain affordability, regardless of global price fluctuations in raw materials**. So, statement I is not correct.**
● **Di-ammonium Phosphate**, popularly known as DAP, is a preferred fertilizer in India because it contains both Nitrogen and Phosphorus which are primary macro-nutrients and part of 18 essential plant nutrients. o Fertilizer grade DAP Contains **18% Nitrogen and 46% Phosphorus (P2O5).** DAP is
manufactured by reacting Ammonia with Phosphoric acid under controlled conditions in fertilizer plants.
● Urea, is provided to the farmers at a **statutorily notiﬁed Maximum Retail Price (MRP).** The difference between the delivered cost of urea at farm gate and net market realization by the urea units is given as a **subsidy to the urea manufacturer/importer** by the Government of India. Accordingly, all farmers are being supplied **urea at subsidized rates.**
○ **Urea** is excluded from the NBS scheme. It is subsidized at a **fixed rate per kilogram**, while DAP is subsidized under the Nutrient Based Subsidy (NBS) scheme. **So, statement II is** **correct.**
● The Government has introduced a **Direct Benefit Transfer (DBT) system** in Fertilizers w.e.f. October 2016, under which, 100% subsidy on various fertilizer grades is released to the fertilizer companies on the **basis of actual sales made by the retailers to the beneficiaries,** verified using **Point of Sale (PoS) devices** installed at each retailer shop. It is **not disbursed in advance based** **on anticipated demand**. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box:** **What are Macronutrients and Micronutrients?**
● Plants need both Macronutrients and Micronutrients to survive.
● **Macronutrients:** o Macronutrients are elements needed in relatively large amounts by plants
**(more than 10m mole kg-1 of plant dry weight).** o Macronutrients include nitrogen (N), phosphorus (P), potassium (K), calcium
(Ca), magnesium (Mg), and sulfur (S),
● **Micronutrients:** o Micronutrients/Trace elements are required in relatively small quantities i.e.,
**less than 10m mole kg-1 of plant dry weight.** o Micronutrients include iron (Fe), manganese (Mn), boron (B), zinc (Zn),
copper (Cu), molybdenum (Mo), chlorine (Cl), and nickel (Ni).
● Plants obtain carbon (C), hydrogen (H), and oxygen (O) from air and water, while the other essential nutrients are derived from the soil. **Relevance**: The parliamentary Standing Committee on Chemicals and Fertilizers has recently recommended the Union Fertilizers Ministry to seek additional funds for the sector at the revised estimate stage to ensure that subsidy schemes for farmers are not negatively affected.

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: IPR, Institutions and Policies for IPR
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
**Statement I:** A declining Import Coverage Ratio (ICR) may increase a country's vulnerability to external shocks and balance of payments crises.
**Statement II:** A declining ICR reflects a reduction in the foreign exchange reserves available to cover imports.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?

options.a: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct and Statement II explains Statement I
options.b: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct but Statement II does not explain Statement I
options.c: Statement I is correct but Statement II is incorrect
options.d: Statement I is incorrect but Statement II is t

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Import Coverage Ratio (ICR)** is a crucial economic indicator that measures a **country's ability to** **pay for its imports** using its foreign exchange reserves. It is a metric that determines the extent to
which a country can finance its imports **without relying on external borrowing or running into a** **balance of payment crisis**. The ICR is calculated by dividing a country's foreign exchange reserves by its total imports. **So, statement I is correct.**
○ Import Coverage Ratio = Foreign Exchange Reserves / Total Imports.
● The **higher the ratio, the better the country's ability** to pay for its imports and maintain its trade balance. The best option to improve a country's ICR is to **increase its foreign exchange reserves.** This can be achieved through various measures such as boosting exports, attracting foreign investment, and reducing imports. A country can also **borrow from external sources** to increase its foreign exchange reserves, but this should be done cautiously to avoid debt distress.
● On the other hand, countries with **lower or declining import coverage ratios** and international reserves are **more vulnerable to external shocks. So, statement II is correct.**
● The ICR and the international reserves are vital indicators of an economy's resilience to external shocks. Countries with higher import coverage ratios and international reserves are **more resilient** **to external shocks** such as a sudden drop in exports or a rise in global oil prices. **So, both Statement I and Statement II are correct and statement II is the correct explanation for** **Statement I.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.** **Relevance**: India’s foreign exchange reserves have seen a net improvement, with the country’s reserves standing at $676.3 billion as of April 4, 2025, providing an import cover of approximately 11 months.

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: IPR, Institutions and Policies for IPR
questions.questionText:
Which one of the following notifies the SCOMET
(Special Chemicals, Organisms, Materials,
Equipment and Technologies) list in India?

options.a:
Department for Promotion of Industry and
Internal Trade (DPIIT)

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Special Chemicals, Organisms, Materials, Equipment, and Technologies (**SCOMET**) list aims to regulate the export of dual-use items that can be used for both civilian and military applications, **particularly** **those that could contribute to the development of weapons of mass destruction (WMDs)** and their delivery systems.
● It is **notified by the Directorate General of Foreign Trade (DGFT)** under Appendix 3 to Schedule 2 of the ITC (HS) Classification of Export and Import Items. It is governed by the DGFT under the Ministry of Commerce and Industry, Government of India.
● **It is governed by Chapter IVA of the Foreign Trade (Development & Regulation) Act, 1992**, as amended in 2010. This chapter provides the legal basis for export control of dual-use items and outlines penalties for non-compliance. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: IPR, Institutions and Policies for IPR
questions.questionText:
Which of the following types of land were widely
exempted from land ceiling laws ?
I. 	 Plantations
II. 	 Religious institutions
III. 	 Charitable institutions
IV. 	 Educational institutions
Select the correct answer using the code given
below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● In the land reforms undertaken after the independence, certain types of land were **exempted from** **land ceiling laws** under land reform policies. These included **plantations** (e.g., tea, coffee), **religious** **and charitable institutions, educational institutions,** etc. These exemptions **varied across state** but were widely present, and often criticized for diluting the intended egalitarian impact of the reforms. It created **opportunities for evasion** through the transfer of lands to these exempted categories. **So,** **points I, II, III and IV are correct.**
● The implementation of land reforms in India exhibited significant **regional disparities**. While certain states such as Kerala and West Bengal achieved comparatively effective outcomes, many others **struggled with challenges** that undermined reform efforts. These included political resistance, outdated or incomplete land records, and administrative inefficiency or apathy.
● Consequently, the objectives of land ceiling enforcement and equitable land redistribution were **only** **partially realized**, contributing to the persistence of agrarian inequalities across several parts of the country. **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box:**
**What measures were undertaken as a part of the land reforms?**
● After Independence, the **Indian** **National** **Congress** **(INC)** appointed the **Agrarian Reforms Committee** headed by **J.C**. **Kumarapppa**, to conduct a study of the agrarian relations prevailing in the country. Based on the recommendation of the committee, states enacted legislation for the abolition of intermediary tenures in the 1950s.
● **Bhoodan and Gramdan Movements (1951)**: It was launched by Acharya Vinoba Bhave, in Andhra Pradesh, and had the purpose of appealing to landowning classes to donate their surplus land to the poor.
o It created awareness about the issue of landlessness and brought it to the
forefront of public discourse.
● **The Emergence of Gramdan (1955):** It was also started by Vinoba Bhave to establish **s**elf-governing and self-reliant village communities where the resources, including land, would be collectively owned and managed.
● **Five-Year Plans (FYPs):** The basic orientation of land policy set by the FYPs was to provide a framework for economic development and political stability.
o **Objectives**: The objectives were to remove such impediments in
agricultural production arising from the agrarian structure inherited from the past and eliminate all elements of exploitation and social injustice within the agrarian system. **Relevance**: The Indian government had earmarked financial incentives to promote land-related reforms across states, allocating Rs 10,000 crore for these efforts in rural and urban areas during the financial year 2024-25.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
In the context of sugarcane pricing in India, which
one of the following statements is correct?

options.a:
The Fair and Remunerative Price (FRP)
is recommended by the Union Ministry of
Commerce and Industry.
options.b:
State Advised Price (SAP) of sugarcane is
generally lower than the FRP.
options.c:
The FRP is fixed before the commencement
of sugar season.
options.d:
Every State is mandated to pay the SAP under
the Sugarcane (Control) Order, 1966.

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The amendment to the Sugarcane (Control) Order, 1966 replaced the concept of Statutory Minimum Price (SMP) of sugarcane with the **‘Fair and Remunerative Price (FRP)’ of sugarcane**.
● The cane price is announced by the Central Government after approval by the Cabinet Committee on Economic Affairs on the basis of the **recommendations of the** **Commission for Agricultural** **Costs and Prices (CACP)**.
○ FRP is the **minimum price that sugar mills are supposed to pay** to the farmers for purchase of sugarcane. FRP is decided on recommendations of **the Commission for** **Agriculture Costs and Prices (CACP)** in consultations with the State Government and other stakeholders. ○ FRP of sugarcane is fixed **before the commencement of Sugar Season** to ensure guaranteed price to sugarcane growers. ○ FRP is linked to a **basic recovery rate of sugar**, with a premium payable to farmers for higher recoveries, to ensure that higher sugar recoveries are adequately rewarded.
● Some States determine their own **State Advised Price (SAP) which is generally higher than FRP** and it is announced by key sugarcane producing States namely Uttar Pradesh, Punjab, Haryana & Uttarakhand to account for regional variations in production costs, agro-climatic conditions, and sociopolitical considerations.
● **Unlike FRP, SAP is not mandated under any central legislation** and is often criticized for burdening sugar mills, especially when not linked to sugar recovery or market prices. **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
**What role does the Essential Commodities Act 1955 have in controlling sugarcane** **pricing in India?**
● Sugarcane and sugar are covered under the **Essential Commodities Act 1955.** It empowers the regulation of the Sugar Sector.
○ **The Sugarcane (Control) Order, 1966 was issued under Section 3 of** **the Essential Commodities Act, 1955.** ○ **The Sugarcane (Control) Order 1966** gives powers to fix FRP of sugarcane, ensure payment of cane dues to farmers. It has provisions to establish sugar factories. Hence, the powers to fix FRP of sugarcane are derived from the **Essential Commodities Act, 1955.**
**Relevance**: The Central Government has recently approved an increase in the Fair and Remunerative Price (FRP) of sugarcane to ₹355 per quintal for the 2025-26 sugar season.

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: IPR, Institutions and Policies for IPR
questions.questionText:
With reference to pharmaceutical pricing in India,
consider the following statements:
I. The National List of Essential Medicines
(NLEM) is governed by the provisions of the
Drugs (Prices Control) Order, 2013.
II. - The National Pharmaceutical Pricing
Authority (NPPA) fixes the price ceiling for
the medicines listed in the National List of
Essential Medicines (NLEM).
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Pharmaceutical pricing in India is regulated to ensure **availability of essential medicines at** **affordable prices**. The key mechanism for this is the **National List of Essential Medicines (NLEM)**,
which is periodically revised to reflect health priorities.
● The NLEM, notified by the Ministry of Health and Family Welfare, is incorporated into **Schedule-I of** **the Drugs (Prices Control) Order, 2013** by the Department of Pharmaceuticals. This legal framework makes sure that drugs listed in NLEM are subject to price regulation. Thus, DPCO 2013 serves as the legal framework that regulates the pricing of medicines listed under the NLEM. **So,** **statement I is correct.**
● After inclusion in the NLEM and Schedule-I of the DPCO, the NPPA fixes a **ceiling price** for each listed medicine. **Manufacturers cannot sell these drugs at prices higher than the notified ceiling** (plus applicable local taxes). Any violation requires manufacturers to deposit the overcharged amount with interest. This mechanism ensures affordability of essential drugs and prevents exploitative pricing practices. **So, statement II is correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Economy
questions.microTopic: International Organisations (WTO, IMF, World Bank, RCEP, FTAs, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Which of the following currencies are included
in the basket of IMF’s Special Drawing Rights
(SDR)?
I. - Euro
II. 	 Renminbi
III. 	 Yen
IV. 	 Pound Sterling
Select the correct answer using the code given
below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Special Drawing Rights (SDR)** is an international reserve asset created by the **International** **Monetary Fund (IMF)** in 1969 to supplement the official reserves of its member countries. The SDR is **not a currency**. It is **a potential claim on the freely usable currencies** of IMF members. As such, SDRs can provide a country with liquidity.
● The IMF defined the SDR as **equivalent to a fractional amount of gold** that was equivalent to one US dollar. The value of the SDR is based on a basket of five currencies:
○ **U.S. dollar** ○ **Euro. So, point I is correct.** ○ **Chinese Renminbi. So, point II is correct.** ○ **Japanese Yen. So, point III is correct.** ○ **British Pound Sterling. So, point IV is correct.**
● The IMF reviews the SDR basket **every five years**, or earlier if warranted, to ensure that it reflects the relative importance of currencies in the world’s trading and financial systems. The SDR value in terms of the US dollar is **determined daily** based on the spot exchange rates observed at around noon London time. **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** According to the Reserve Bank of India’s recent report, India's Special Drawing Rights (SDRs) declined by $66 million to $18.802 billion in early July.

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: IPR, Institutions and Policies for IPR
questions.questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
Five Year Plan Major outcome
I. - Second Primary focus on speedy industrialization
II. - Fourth Nationalisation of major banks
III. - Fifth Launch of Minimum Needs Programme
IV. - Sixth Beginning of economic liberalisation
V. - Seventh Make India a self reliant economy
How many of the pairs given above are correctly
matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Since Independence, a program of **Five Year Plans** (FYPs) was launched in India in order to ensure the rapid growth and development of the economy. The FYPS in India were formulated by the erstwhile Planning Commission in India which was set up in 1950. Since 2014, the Planning Commission has been replaced by a new institution named NITI (National Institution for Transforming India) Aayog.
● The first Five-year Plan was launched in 1951 and two subsequent five-year plans were formulated till 1965, when there was a break because of the Indo-Pakistan Conflict. Two successive years of drought, devaluation of the currency, a general rise in prices and erosion of resources disrupted the planning process and after three Annual Plans between 1966 and 1969, the fourth Five-year plan was started in 1969.
○ **First Plan:** The period of the first five year plan was 1951-56. The plan was based on the HarrodDomar Model and was presented to the Parliament of India by the first Indian Prime Minister, Pandit Jawaharlal Nehru. The focus of the plan was on **food self sufficiency**, price control, power and transport. ○ **Second Plan:** The duration of the second five year plan was from 1956 to 61. The plan is also called the **Mahalanobis Plan**. The **primary focus of the plan was on speedy** **industrialization.** **So, pair I is correctly matched.** ○ **Third Plan:** The time period of the third plan was 1961 to 1966. The plan focused on the growth of industry and agriculture. The aim was to make India a **“Self Reliant” and “Self** **Generating” economy**. The plan was not able to achieve its target due to unexpected situations such as Chinese aggression, Indo-Pak war and a bad monsoon. The target growth rate was 5.6%, but the actual growth rate was 2.4%. **So, pair V is not correctly matched.** ○ **Fourth Plan (1969** **−** **74):** The stress of the fourth five year plan was on bringing about agriculture growth in India. The plan aimed at “Growth with Stability” and “Progressive Achievement of Self Reliance”. The **Green Revolution advanced agricultural production** in India. **14 major banks in India were also nationalized** during this period. The target growth rate for the period was 5.6% and the actual growth rate of 3.3% was achieved. **So, pair** **II is correctly matched.** ○ **Fifth Plan:** The period of the fifth plan was from 1974 to 79. The focus of the plan was on “poverty alleviation” and “attainment of self reliance in agriculture and defense”. The **Minimum** **Needs Programme** was started during this period. The plan was terminated in its fourth year by the Janta Party. The target growth rate was 4.4% and the actual growth rate was 5%. **So, pair** **III is correctly matched.** ○ **Sixth Plan:** The period of the plan was from 1980 to 1985. The emphasis of the plan was on agriculture and infrastructure. **Economic Liberalisation also began during the sixth five** **year plan**. The target and actual growth rate during the plan was 5.2% and 5.4% respectively. **So, pair IV is correctly matched.** **So, only four of the pairs given above are correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
**More on FYPs:**
● **Three Yearly Plans (1966 – 1969):** The emphasis of the three annual plans was on agriculture. The shortage of food and agriculture crisis led to the formulation of a new agriculture strategy. The strategy focused upon the need for the **high yielding variety** **of seeds**, use of fertilizers, better irrigation facilities and conservation of soil in order to improve agricultural productivity.
● **Rolling Plan (1978** **−** **80):** Two sixth plans (rolling plans) were introduced by the Janta Party from 1978 to 1983 and the Congress government in 1980. The first plan was in operation for two years only. The benefit of the rolling plans was that they were **flexible and the targets could change** according to the changing conditions in the country. The demerit of the plans was that the targets kept on getting revised and therefore were difficult to achieve.
● **Seventh Plan (1985** **−** **90):**  It stressed upon **growth in agricultural production**, economic productivity and employment generation. India recorded 6% growth rate during this period as against the targeted growth rate of 5%.
● **Eighth Plan (**1992 to 1997): Its aim was to generate **employment**, improve agricultural productivity, check population growth, strengthen  infrastructure and to alleviate poverty.
● **Ninth Plan (1997** **−** **2002):** It emphasised improving the quality of life of the people of the country through economic growth. The plan focused upon **social justice and** **equity**. The aim was to remove the inequalities which were still widespread in the society.
● **Tenth Plan (2002** **−** **2007):** Recognising that economic growth cannot be the only objective of the national plan, the Tenth Plan had set ‘monitorable targets’ for few key indicators (11) of development besides 8 % growth target. The targets included **reduction in gender gaps in literacy and wage rate**, reduction in Infant & maternal mortality rates, improvement in literacy, access to potable drinking water, cleaning of major polluted rivers, etc.
● **Eleventh Plan (2007-2012):** The broad vision for 11th Plan included several inter related components like **rapid growth reducing poverty & creating employment** **opportunities** , access to essential services in health & education, specially for the poor, extension if employment opportunities using National Rural Employment Guarantee Programme , environmental sustainability , reduction of gender inequality etc.
● **Twelfth Plan:** It aimed to attain an average growth rate of 8.2% as compared to 9% decided earlier. The targeted growth rate has been brought down in light of the adverse domestic and global economic scenario. The strategy of the twelfth plan is **“faster,** **sustainable and more inclusive growth”.**

questions.subject: Economy
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference
to the Nominal Effective Exchange Rate (NEER)
and the Real Effective Exchange Rate (REER):
I. - Unlike REER, NEER adjusts for inflation
differences between countries.
II. 	 A rise in NEER indicates appreciation.
III. 	 The rupee is considered to be fairly valued if
the REER is close to 100.
Which of the statements given above is/are
correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Nominal Effective Exchange Rate (NEER)** is the **weighted average of a currency’s bilateral** **exchange rates** with multiple trading partner currencies.
○ NEER reflects the **nominal strength** of a currency but does not adjust for **inflation or price** **level differences**. ○ A rise in NEER indicates nominal appreciation, while a decline signals depreciation.  **So,** **statement II is correct.**
● **Real Effective Exchange Rate (REER)** is an improvement over NEER, as it adjusts for **relative price** **levels** (inflation) between domestic and foreign economies.
○ **Calculated as:** NEER × (Domestic Price Index ÷ Foreign Price Index). ○ It is a **purchasing power parity (PPP)-adjusted** measure of competitiveness.
● NEER is a nominal measure that reflects the weighted average exchange rate of a currency against a basket of others, **without adjusting for inflation**. Whereas, REER adjusts NEER for **inflation** **differentials** between the domestic economy and its trading partners, making it a better indicator of **real purchasing power** and **trade competitiveness. So, statement I is not correct.**
● **The rupee is considered to be fairly valued if the REER is close to 100** or the base-year value. Other things remaining the same, higher domestic inflation vis-à-vis its trade partners will reflect in appreciation of the REER and hence there is a view that the nominal exchange rate should depreciate to keep it at base-year levels. **So, statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.** **Relevance**: The Real Effective Exchange Rate (REER) index of the rupee touched a record 108.14 recently.

questions.subject: Economy
questions.questionText:
Arrange the following countries in descending
order in terms of Gross Domestic Product based
on Purchasing Power Parity:
I. - India
II. 	 United States of America (USA)
III. 	 China
IV. 	 Russia
Select the correct answer using the code given
below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
Purchasing power parity (PPP) is a popular macroeconomic analysis metric used to **compare economic** **productivity and standards of living** between countries. It involves an economic theory that compares different countries' currencies through **a "basket of goods" approach.** It is the exchange rate at which one nation's currency would be converted into another to purchase the same and same amounts of a large group of products.
● **China is the largest economy in the world by Gross Domestic Product based on PPP** in 2025, according to the October 2024 edition of **the International Monetary Fund (IMF)’s World** **Economic Outlook**. With a projected GDP (PPP) of $39.438 trillion, it stands at approximately 1.3 times that of the United States.
● The **United States of America (USA),** recognized as the **world’s largest economy by nominal** **GDP, holds the second position in terms of GDP based on PPP** in 2025. According to the IMF, the country’s PPP-adjusted GDP is projected to reach $30.337 trillion in 2025.
● **India has consistently held the position of the third-largest economy in the world** based on GDP measured by PPP. In terms of **nominal GDP, India ranks fifth** globally. According to the IMF’s estimates, India’s PPP-adjusted GDP is projected to reach $17.365 trillion in 2025 and is expected to climb to $24.015 trillion by 2029.
● **Russia** is the **fourth largest economy** in the world in GDP (PPP) terms. The IMF s latest estimates for 2025 estimate Russia’s GDP (PPP) at $7,130 billion.
● The remaining countries in the top 10 rankings for GDP based on PPP include **Japan, Germany,** **Indonesia, Brazil, France, the United Kingdom (UK), and Italy.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: IPR, Institutions and Policies for IPR
questions.questionText:
Consider the following:
I. - Geographical Indication (GI) of Goods
II. 	 Patent
III. 	 Copyright
IV. 	 Trademark
How many of the above are issued by the
Department for Promotion of Industry and Internal
Trade (DPIIT) in India?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Intellectual property (IP)** refers to **creations of the mind**, such as inventions; literary and artistic works; designs; and symbols, names and images used in commerce. IP is **protected in law** by, for example, patents, copyright and trademarks, which enable people to earn recognition or financial benefit from what they invent or create. By striking the right balance between the interests of innovators and the wider public interest, the IP system aims to foster an environment in which creativity and innovation can flourish.
● **Geographical Indications (GI)** **of Goods:** It is defined as that aspect of industrial property which refers to the geographical indication referring to a country or to a place situated therein as being the country or place of origin of that product. The **Geographical Indication Registry**, **a division of the** **DPIIT**, is responsible for issuing GI tags, further safeguarding these unique products and their geographical origins. **So, point I is correct.**
● **Patent:** It is a **statutory right for an invention** granted for a limited period of time to the patentee by the Government, in exchange of full disclosure of his invention for excluding others, from making, using, selling, importing the patented product or process for producing that product for those purposes without his consent. The **Indian Patent Office (IPO),** a part of the office of the **Controller General** **of Patents, Designs and Trademarks (CGPDTM)** under the **Department for Promotion of** **Industry and Internal Trade (DPIIT), Ministry of Commerce and Industry**, Government of India is responsible for the grant of patents in India. **So, point II is correct.**
● **Copyright:** It is a legal term used to describe the rights that **creators have over their literary and** **artistic works**. Works covered by copyright range from books, music, paintings, sculpture and films, to computer programs, databases, advertisements, maps and technical drawings.
o Copyright ensures certain **minimum safeguards of the rights of authors** over their
creations, thereby protecting and rewarding creativity. Unlike the case with patents, **copyright** **protects the expressions** **and not the ideas**. There is no copyright protection for ideas, procedures, methods of operation or mathematical concepts as such. o In India, copyright is issued by the **Copyright Office**, which is part of the **DPIIT**. While the
Copyright Office was **previously under the Ministry of Human Resource Development** **(now Ministry of Education),** it was transferred to DPIIT.  **So, point III is correct.**
● **Trademark:** It is a sign capable of **distinguishing the goods or services** of one enterprise from those of other enterprises. Trademarks date back to ancient times when artisans used to put their signature or "mark" on their products. In India, trademarks are issued by the **Trade Marks Registry**,
which is part of the **Office of the Controller General of Patents, Designs, and Trade Marks** **(CGPDTM),** an agency under the **DPIIT**.  **So, point IV is correct.**
● The other forms of IP include the following:
o **Industrial Design:** Issued by the Controller-General of Patents, Designs, and Trade Marks,
under DPIIT o **Plant Variety Protection (PVP):** Issued by the Protection of Plant Varieties and Farmers'
Rights Authority (PPV&FRA), under Ministry of Agriculture and Farmers Welfare **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
● **Geographical Indications (GI)** **of Goods:** o Under Articles 1 (2) and 10 of the **Paris Convention for the Protection of**
**Industrial Property,** geographical indications are covered as an element of IPRs. o India enacted the **Geographical Indications of Goods (Registration &**
**Protection) Act, 1999** which provides a comprehensive legal framework for the registration and protection of geographical indications (GIs) in India. o In India, a GI tag is **valid for 10 years** from the date of registration. After that,
it can be **renewed indefinitely** in blocks of 10 years each.
● **Patents:** o **The Paris Convention for the Protection of Industrial Property (1883)**
is an international treaty that ensures equal IP rights for foreign and domestic applicants, grants a right of priority for patent filings, and promotes cooperation among member countries to protect industrial property. **India ratified** this convention in 1998. o The patent system in India is governed by the **Patents Act, 1970** as amended
by the Patents (Amendment) Act, 2005 and the Patents Rules, 2003. o Patent protection is a **territorial right** and therefore it is effective only within
the territory of India. There is no concept of global/world patent. o The term of every patent granted is **20 years** from the date of filing of
application.
● **Copyright:** o **The Berne Convention for the Protection of Literary and Artistic**
**Works (1886)** is an international treaty that ensures **automatic copyright** **protection** for literary and artistic works across member countries. **India** **ratified** the Convention on April 28, 1928. o In India, the **Copyright Act, 1957** protects original literary, dramatic, musical
and artistic works and cinematograph films and sound recordings from unauthorized uses. o In India, the validity period of copyright protection generally extends for the
**author's lifetime plus 60 years** from the beginning of the calendar year following the author's death. This rule applies to literary, dramatic, musical, and artistic works.
● **Trademark:**  In India, a registered trademark is initially valid for **10 years** from the date of application. This validity can be **extended indefinitely** through renewals, each lasting for another 10 years
o The **Paris Convention for the Protection of Industrial Property,**
**established in 1883**, provides a framework for international cooperation on **trademark** protection, among other aspects of industrial property. o **The Trade Marks Act, 1999** is India’s principal legislation for the registration,
protection, and enforcement of trademarks for goods and services. o For cinematographic films, sound recordings, photographs, and works of
government or international organizations, the copyright is valid for **60 years** **from the date of publication**.
**Relevance**: Recently, the U.S. had once again placed India on its 'priority watch list', stating that India remains one of the world's most challenging major economies for the protection and enforcement of Intellectual Property Rights (IPRs).

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
Act Feature
I.
Agricultural Produce (Grading and Marking) Act,
Provides for AGMARK certification
II. - Essential Commodities Act,
Preventive detention for obstructing the supply of essential commodities
III.
Prevention of Black Marketing and Maintenance of Supplies of Essential Commodities Act,
Regulate the trade and commerce of essential commodities
How many of the pairs given above are correctly
matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Agricultural Produce (Grading and Marking) Act, 1937** was enacted to ensure the **standardization and quality control** of agricultural and allied products through **grading and** **marking systems**, most notably under the **AGMARK** certification. **So, pair I is correctly matched.**
○ Under **Section 2(a)** of the Act, the term **"agricultural produce"** is defined to include: “all produce of agriculture or horticulture and all articles of food or drink wholly or partly manufactured from any such produce, and **fleeces and the skins of animals**.”
● Under the provisions of the **Essential Commodities Act, 1955**, the Government is vested with the authority to **regulate the production, supply, distribution, trade, and commerce** of essential commodities in the interest of the general public. **So, pair II is not correctly matched.**
● The **Prevention of Black Marketing and Maintenance of Supplies of Essential Commodities** **Act, 1980** does allow for the preventive detention of individuals who are deemed to be obstructing the supply of essential commodities.
○ This Act is **separate from the Essential Commodities Act, 1955,** though both are related to the **regulation and control of essential goods.** The 1980 Act specifically focuses on preventing black marketing and maintaining supplies by allowing for detention in certain cases. **So,** **pair III is correctly matched.** **So, only two of the pairs given above are correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.** **Relevance**: The District Magistrate of U.T. of Chandigarh, recently banned hoarding of essential commodities (rice, wheat, sugar, fuel, etc.) under the Essential Commodities Act, 1955.

questions.subject: International Relations
questions.microTopic: India and the World (USA, Russia, Middle East, Africa, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference to Fish production in India:
I. - The fisheries and aquaculture production accounts for more than 10% to the Gross Domestic Product (GDP).
II. 	 The share of inland fisheries is more than that of the share of marine fisheries.
III. 	 Tamil Nadu ranks first in terms of fishing and aquaculture production in India.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● India is the **second largest fish producing country** with around **8% share** in global fish production. The fisheries and aquaculture production contributes around **1% to India’s Gross Domestic Product** **(GDP) and over 5% to the agricultural GDP**. **So, statement I is not correct.**
● Traditionally the Fisheries sector in India has been demarcated into two broad categories such as **marine** **and inland fisheries**. Around **70% of India’s fish production comes from inland waters**, of which nearly 65% comes from aquaculture. The share of marine fisheries in total output has been falling significantly over the years compared to inland fisheries. **So, statement II is correct.**
● **Andhra Pradesh** is the top State in fishing and aquaculture and accounts for 40.9% of the share in the sector followed by **West Bengal, Odisha and Bihar. So, statement III is not correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.** **Relevance**: The Union Budget 2025-26, proposed the highest ever total annual budgetary support of Rs. 2,703.67 crores for the fisheries sector.

questions.subject: Economy
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to electronic-National Agriculture Market (e-NAM):
I. - It is managed by the Small Farmers’ Agribusiness Consortium (SFAC).
II. 	 Its transactions are officially recorded in the books of the local mandi APMC.
III. 	 Tulsi leaves, dried fenugreek leaves and asafoetida can be traded on e-NAM.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **electronic-National Agriculture Market (e-NAM)**: It was launched in **2016**.  It is a national electronic trading portal connecting APMC mandis across India to create a unified market for agricultural goods. This expansion aims to increase the platform’s commodity coverage, improve opportunities for farmers and traders, and enhance transparency and fair trading practices.
● The **Small Farmers' Agribusiness Consortium (SFAC)** is the nodal agency responsible for implementing and managing the e-NAM (National Agriculture Market) platform. **So, statement I is** **correct.**
○ SFAC plays a crucial role in rolling out and operating this pan-India electronic trading portal,
which aims to integrate existing Agricultural Produce Market Committee (APMC) mandis and create a unified national market for agricultural commodities.
● Even when a farmer accepts an online offer through the e-NAM (Electronic National Agriculture Market) platform, **the transaction is officially recorded in the books of the local mandi** APMC. e-NAM acts as a bridge connecting farmers to a larger market, but the physical mandi remains the point of sale and the entity responsible for maintaining transaction records and collecting market fees. **So,** **statement II is correct.**
● The Union Ministry of Agriculture has recently approved 10 more commodities for trading on the eNAM (electronic-National Agriculture Market) platform, increasing the total to **231 tradable items**. The new additions are **dried Tulsi leaves**, Besan (chickpea flour), wheat flour, chana sattu (roasted chickpea flour), water chestnut flour, **asafoetida, dried fenugreek leaves**, baby corn, dragon fruit and water chestnut. **So, statement III is correct.**
● The **e-NAM platform** also facilitates trading of both **primary agricultural produce** and **value-** **added products**. By expanding to include the secondary or value-added items, e-NAM enables Farmer Producer Organizations (FPOs) to market processed products and helps formalize trade in the sector, supporting better market access and price realization for farmers. **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Economy
questions.questionText:
Which of the following are considered as the sources of human capital?
I. - Investment in education
II. 	 Investment in health
III. 	 Expenditure on migration
IV. 	 Expenditure on acquiring information
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Human Capital:** is the **economic value of a worker's experience and skills.** It is **an intangible asset** not listed on a company's balance sheet and perceived to increase productivity and profitability.
● **Investment in education:** It is considered as one of the main sources of human capital. Investments in education **convert human beings into human capital**; human capital represents enhanced labour productivity, which is an acquired ability and an outcome of deliberate investment decisions with an expectation that it will increase future income sources. **So, point I is correct.**
● **Investment in health:** It is considered as an important input for the development of a nation as much as it is important for the development of an individual. A sick labourer without access to medical facilities is compelled to abstain from work and there is **loss of productivity**. Hence, expenditure on health is an important source of human capital formation. Health expenditure directly increases the supply of a healthy labour force and is, thus, a source of human capital formation. **So, point II is correct.**
● **Expenditure on migration:** People migrate **in search of jobs** that fetch them **higher salaries** than what they may get in their native places. Unemployment is the reason for the rural-urban migration in India.
○ Technically qualified persons, like engineers and doctors, migrate to other countries because of higher salaries that they may get in such countries. ○ Migration in both these cases involves cost of transport, higher cost of living in the migrated places and psychic costs of living in a strange socio-cultural setup. The **enhanced earnings in** **the new place outweigh the costs of migration;** hence, expenditure on migration is also a source of human capital formation. **So, point III is correct.**
● **Expenditure incurred for acquiring information:** People spend to **acquire information** **relating** **to the labour market and other markets** like education and health.
○ For example, people want to know the level of salaries associated with various types of jobs, whether the educational institutions provide the right type of employable skills and at what cost. ○ This information is **necessary to make decisions** regarding investments in human capital as well as for efficient utilisation of the acquired human capital stock. ○ Expenditure incurred for acquiring information relating to the labour market and other markets is also a source of human capital formation. **So, point IV is correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.** **Relevance**: According to experts, higher education institutions in India require “autonomy with accountability and not control through compliance” if the country is to radically improve its human capital.

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: IPR, Institutions and Policies for IPR
questions.questionText:
Consider the following:
I. - Conducting survey of Pradhan Mantri Mudra Yojna
II. 	 Preparation of Retail Price Indices for essential commodities in urban areas
III.	 Organising training programmes for Price Collectors from States
IV.	 Publishing of Consumer Food Price Index (CFPI) for rural  areas
Which of the above are the functions of the Labour Bureau?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The Labour Bureau, established in 1920, is an attached office under the **Ministry of Labour &** **Employment** and is responsible for the collection, collation and dissemination of labour, employment and price statistics. The functions/activities of Labour Bureau can be classified under **five major heads:**
● **Labour Intelligence:** Construction and maintenance of various series of index numbers: ○ Consumer Price Index Numbers (CPI) - Industrial Workers ○ Consumer Price Index Numbers (CPI)- Agricultural Labourers and Rural Labourers ○ Wage Rate Indices in respect of industries covered under the Occupational Wage Surveys. ○ **Retail Price Indices for 31 Essential Commodities** in Urban Areas. **So, point II is** **correct.** ○ Provisioning of administrative statistics on Absenteeism, Labour Turnover, Labour Cost, Employment, Earnings, Industrial Disputes, etc.
● The Consumer Food Price Index (CFPI) for Rural (R) areas is published on a monthly basis by the **National Statistical Office (NSO) and not by the Labour Bureau**. **So, point IV is not correct.**
● **Labour Research:** ○ Conducting research studies/ surveys and bringing out publications on labour related matters in the organized and unorganized sector. ○ These include studies on Unorganised sector, SC/ST Labour, Women Workers surveys, etc.; Evaluation Studies on Implementation of Minimum Wages Act.; Occupational Wage Survey; Rural Labour Enquiry; **Annual Survey of Industries**; Digest of Indian Labour Research; Statistical Profile on Women Labour; Quarterly Employment Surveys; Area Frame Survey; Survey on **Pradhan Mantri Mudra Yojna**, etc. **So, point I is correct.**
● **Training:** Regular training programmes are organised for: ○ **Price Collectors and Price Supervisors** from various States and Union Territories. **So,** **point III is correct.** ○ **Senior Officers from States and Union Territories** dealing with Labour Statistics collected under various Labour Laws (State DES and Labour Commissionerate officials)
● **Monitoring and Evaluation:** ○ This involves collection, compilation and dissemination of **statistical information on various** **aspects of labour based on statutory and voluntary returns** under different Labour Acts and surveys conducted.
● **Regular Publications:** ○ Indian Labour Journal (Monthly), Indian Labour Statistics (Annual), Pocket Book of Labour Statistics (Annual) and Indian Labour Year Book (Annual) giving authoritative and up-to-date statistics on various facets of Labour and on current Labour scene in the country. **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Economy
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
**Statement I:** The Central Government has reduced the basic customs duty on crude edible oils.
**Statement II:** Lowering import duty helps reduce the landed cost and retail prices of edible oils in the domestic market. **Statement III:** The reduced import duty encourages domestic refining and maintains fair compensation for farmers.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?

options.a: Both Statement II and Statement III are correct and both of them explain Statement I
options.b: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct and Statement II explains Statement I
options.c: Only one of the Statements II and III is correct and that explains Statement I
options.d: Neither Statement II nor Statement III is correct

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The Government of India, to ensure availability of edible oil to consumers at fair prices, has recently **reduced the Basic Custom duty (BCD)** on Crude Palm Oil, Crude Soyabean oil and Crude Sunflower Oil from 20% to 10% with effect from 30th May, 2025. **So, statement I is correct.**
● Import duty on edible oils is one of the important factors that **impacted landed cost of edible oils** and thereby domestic prices. By lowering the import duty on crude oils, the government aims to **reduce** **the landed cost and retail prices of edible oils,** providing relief to consumers and helping to **cool** **overall inflation**. The reduced duty will also **encourage domestic refining** and maintain fair compensation for farmers. **So, statements II and III are correct.**
● Further, after this reduction in BCD on Crude Palm, Soybean and Sunflower oil which helps to **maintain** **a duty differential** of 19.25% between crude and refined oils to encourage domestic refining capacity utilization and reduce imports of refined oils.
● The revised duty structure will **discourage the import of refined oils** like Palmolein and shift demand towards crude oils, especially crude palm oil. This is expected to **strengthen domestic refining** **capacity and support local processors** while also benefiting farmers. **So, both Statement II and Statement III are correct and both of them explain Statement I** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.** **Relevance**: The central government had recently reduced the basic customs duty (BCD) on imported crude edible oils from 20% to 10%.

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: IPR, Institutions and Policies for IPR
questions.questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
Organisation Works under
I. - National Statistics
Office (NSO)
Ministry of
Finance
II. - Agricultural and Processed Food Products Export Development Authority (APEDA)
Ministry of Commerce and Industry
III. - National Pharmaceutical Pricing Authority (NPPA)
Ministry of Health & Family Welfare
How many of the pairs given above are correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **National Statistical Office (NSO):** It is responsible for coordinating statistical activities and developing the statistical system in the country. It was formed by **merging the Central Statistics** **Office (CSO) and the National Sample Survey Office (NSSO).** It operates under the Ministry of Statistics and Programme Implementation **(MoSPI).** It acts as the nodal agency for developing the statistical system in India, sets norms and standards in statistics, coordinates statistical activities across ministries and state bureaus, advises on statistical methods and analysis, compiles and releases the Index of Industrial Production (IIP), conducts the Annual Survey of Industries (ASI), and provides data to assess the organised manufacturing sector, etc. **So, pair I is not correctly matched.**
● **Agricultural and Processed Food Products Export Development Authority (APEDA):** It was established by the Government of India under the **Agricultural and Processed Food Products** **Export Development Authority Act** passed by the Parliament in December, 1985. The Authority replaced the Processed Food Export Promotion Council (PFEPC). It works under the **Ministry of** **Commerce and Industry.** Its objective is to **develop and promote the export of scheduled** **products, setting the standards** and specifications for the scheduled products, r**egistration of** **exporters** of the scheduled products on payment of required fees, etc. **So, pair II is correctly** **matched.**
● **National Pharmaceutical Pricing Authority (NPPA):** It was set up as an attached office of the **Department of Chemicals and Petrochemicals (now Department of Pharmaceuticals**, under **Ministry of Chemicals and Fertilizers**) on 29th August 1997. The mandate of NPPA is to **ensure** **availability of essential drugs at affordable prices** and ensure affordability without jeopardizing access to such medicines. It is also entrusted with the functions to implement and enforce the provisions of the **Drugs Price Control Order (DPCO), 1995/2013** in accordance with the powers delegated to it. **So, pair III is not correctly matched.** **So, only one of the above pairs is correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.** **Relevance**: The year 2025 marks the 75th anniversary of the National Sample Survey (NSS), a significant milestone for the National Statistics Office (NSO).

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: IPR, Institutions and Policies for IPR
questions.questionText:
With reference to the "One Nation One Ration Card" (ONORC) scheme, consider the following statements:
I. - It allows individuals to access Public Distribution System (PDS) benefits from any Fair Price Shop in the country.
II. 	 It is available to all beneficiaries covered under the National Food Security Act (NFSA), 2013 with Aadhaar Card numbers seeded.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The "**One Nation One Ration Card" (ONORC) scheme** is a national Ration Card portability scheme to ensure food security for all including internal migrants within India. It was launched by the Department of Food & Supplies and Consumer Affairs, **Ministry of Consumer Affairs, Food &** **Public Distribution** in 2018.
● It **enables migrant workers and their family members to access Public Distribution System** **(PDS) benefits** from any Fair Price Shop anywhere in the country, thus ensuring food security through the inter-state portability of ration cards under the National Food Security Act, 2013 (NFSA). **So,** **statement I is correct.**
○ A Fair Price Shop (FPS) is a public ration store licensed under Section 3 of the Essential Commodities Act of 1955. Ration card details and entitlements are available online on any ePoS device in the country.
● The scheme is **eligible to all ration cardholders or beneficiaries covered under the National** **Food Security Act (NFSA), 2013** with Aadhaar Card numbers seeded. **So, statement II is** **correct.**
● It includes both **inter-State and intra-State portability** of ration cards. The ONORC plan has successfully been implemented in **all 36 States/UTs** making food security portable throughout the country.
● The government has launched a mobile app, namely ‘**MERA RATION**’ for acquiring the maximum benefit of the ONORC plan. The mobile app shall provide the user with real-time information. It is available in 13 languages. **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: IPR, Institutions and Policies for IPR
questions.questionText:
Consider the following crops:
I. - Safflower
II. 	 Groundnut
III. 	 Turmeric
IV. 	 Copra
How many of the above are provided with the Minimum Support Price by the Government of India?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The Government of India fixes Minimum Support Prices (MSPs) for **22 mandated agricultural crops** on the basis of the recommendations of the Commission for Agricultural Costs & Prices (CACP), after considering the views of State Governments and Central Ministries/ Departments concerned. The government procures cereals and coarse cereals through Food Corporation of India (FCI) and other designated State Agencies to provide price support to the farmers.
● These crops include 14 Kharif crops, 6 Rabi crops, and 2 commercial crops. Additionally, MSPs are also fixed for Toria and de-husked coconut based on the MSPs of rapeseed & mustard and copra, respectively.
● These crops are: ○ **Kharif Crops** ■ Paddy ■ Jowar ■ Bajra. ■ Ragi ■ Maize ■ Tur (Arhar) ■ Moong ■ Urad ■ Groundnut.**So, point II is correct.** ■ Sunflower Seed ■ Soyabean (Yellow) ■ Sesamum ■ Nigerseed ■ Cotton. ○ **Rabi Crops** ■ Wheat ■ Barley. ■ Gram ■ Masur (Lentil) ■ Rapeseed & Mustard ■ Safflower. **So, point I is correct.** ○ **Commercial Crops** ■ Copra. **So, point IV is correct.** ■ Jute
● **Turmeric is not covered by the FCI**, but some states like Himachal Pradesh have planned to introduce it. **So, point III is not correct.** **So, only three of the above are provided with the Minimum Support Price by the Government of** **India.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Economy
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference to the United Nations Environment Programme Finance Initiative (UNEP FI):
I. - It was the first organisation to engage the finance sector on sustainability.
II. 	 It has developed the Principles for Responsible Banking.
III. 	 It is mandatory for all financial institutions engaged in UNEP projects to be a member of UNEP FI.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
The United Nations Environment Programme Finance Initiative (UNEP FI) is a **global partnership between** **UNEP and over 220 banks, insurers and investors** to understand environmental, social and governance issues in the context of financial performance and sustainable development.
● Founded in **1992**, UNEP FI was the **first organisation to engage the finance sector on** **sustainability** and incubated the Principles for Responsible Investment, now the world’s leading proponent of responsible investment. **So, statement I is correct.**
● Financial institutions work with UNEP FI on **a voluntary basis**, and it is **not mandatory** for the financial institutions to be a member of UNEP FI. **So, statement III is not correct.**
● UNEP FI assists the financial institutions to apply the **industry frameworks and develop practical** **guidance** and tools to position their businesses for the transition to a sustainable and inclusive economy.
● Convened by **a Geneva, Switzerland-based secretariat,** more than 500 banks and insurers with assets exceeding US$170 trillion are individually implementing UNEP FI’s **Principles for Responsible** **Banking** and Principles for Sustainable Insurance. **So, statement II is correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box:**
● The **UNEP FI Principles for Sustainable Insurance (PSI) Initiative** is the largest collaborative initiative between the United Nations and the insurance industry, aimed at propelling environmental, social and economic sustainability.
● The Principles were launched in **Rio de Janeiro in June 2012** to support the aims of the UN Conference on Sustainable Development (Rio+20).
**Relevance**: The United Nations Environment Programme Finance Initiative (UNEP FI) recently participated at the Fourth International Conference for Financing Development (FfD4) held in Spain.

questions.subject: International Relations
questions.microTopic: Global Groupings and Agreements (FTA, Paris Agreement, NPT, QUAD, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Consider the following countries:
I. - United States of America
II. 	 Australia
III. 	 United Kingdom
IV. 	 Mauritius
How many of the above countries have a bilateral Free Trade Agreement (FTA) with India?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Free Trade Agreements (FTAs)** are treaties between two or more countries aimed **at reducing** **or eliminating trade barriers, such as tariffs, quotas and restrictions** on imports and exports. These agreements often include provisions for trade facilitation, intellectual property rights, and investment protections.
○ **India-Australia Economic Cooperation and Trade Agreement (Ind-Aus ECTA)** was signed in December 2022, promoting key sectors like agriculture and minerals. **So, point II is** **correct.** ○ **India and the United Kingdom have signed the Comprehensive Economic and Trade** **Agreement (CETA) in 2025**, a bilateral free trade agreement marking a major milestone in their longstanding partnership. **So, point III is correct.**
○ **India-Mauritius Comprehensive Economic Cooperation and Partnership Agreement** **(CECPA)** signed in 2021, is India’s first trade agreement with an African nation. **So, point IV** **is correct.**
● **India and the United States of America (USA)** are currently engaged in negotiations for a Bilateral Trade Agreement (BTA) with the aim of significantly increasing bilateral trade to $500 billion by 2030. It is **still under negotiation and not yet signed** by the two countries. **So, point I is not correct.** **So, only three of the above countries have a bilateral Free Trade Agreement (FTA) with India.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
● **Preferential Trade Agreements (PTAs):** Unlike FTAs, PTAs offer **selective tariff** **reductions on specific goods**, rather than complete elimination. Some of the examples of such agreement include the India-MERCOSUR PTA and the India-Chile PTA.
● **Comprehensive** **Economic** **Partnerships/Cooperation** **Agreements** **(CEPAs/CECAs):** These agreements go beyond FTAs by including provisions on **investment, services, and regulatory cooperation.** This includes the India-South Korea CEPA and the India-Singapore CECA.
● **Bilateral Investment Treaties (BITs):** BITs focus on protecting and **promoting** **investments** between two countries by ensuring fair treatment and dispute resolution mechanisms. For example, the India-UAE BIT and the India-Uzbekistan BIT.
● **Regional Trade Agreements (RTAs):** These involve multiple countries within a specific region aiming for economic integration. Examples include **the South Asian** **Free Trade Area (SAFTA) and the Asia-Pacific Trade Agreement (APTA).**
**Relevance**: Recently, India and the European Union have set a year-end deadline for the conclusion of the Free Trade Agreement.

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.questionText:
Which of the following statements are correct with regard to the Financial Action Task Force (FATF)?
I. - It does not address issues related to low tax jurisdiction or tax competition.
II. 	 It is not an investigating authority.
III.	 Its recommendations are not binding on the national governments.
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
The **Financial Action Task Force (FATF)** was established by the Group of 7 (G-7) Summit in Paris in 1989 to develop a coordinated international response against money laundering.
● It has set standards and promotes effective implementation of legal, regulatory and operational measures to **combat money laundering and terrorist financing** and other related threats to the integrity of the international financial system.
● The FATF **does not address issues** related to **low tax jurisdiction** or **tax competition.** The FATF mandate focuses only on the fight against laundering of proceeds of crimes and the financing of terrorism. **So, statement I is correct.**
● The FATF is a policy-making body and has **no investigative authority.** In respect to investigating a company and persons involved in money laundering, individuals need to contact their **local** **investigative authorities**.  **So, statement II is correct.**
● The recommendations of the FATF are **not binding** on the national governments.  **So, statement III** **is correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
**Lists under the Financial Action Task Force (FATF):**
● **High-Risk Jurisdictions subject to a Call for Action (i.e. "black list"):** ○ This statement,  (previously called "Public Statement"), identifies countries or jurisdictions with **serious strategic deficiencies to counter money** **laundering**, terrorist financing, and financing of proliferation. ○ For all countries identified as high-risk, the FATF calls on all members and urges all jurisdictions to apply enhanced due diligence, and in the most serious cases, countries are called upon to **apply counter-measures to protect the** **international financial system** from the ongoing money laundering, terrorist financing, and proliferation financing risks emanating from the country.
● **Jurisdictions under Increased Monitoring (i.e. "grey list")** ○ This statement identifies countries that are **actively working with the FATF** **to address strategic deficiencies** in their regimes to counter money laundering, terrorist financing, and proliferation financing. ○ When the FATF places a jurisdiction under increased monitoring, it means the **country has committed to swiftly resolve the identified strategic** **deficiencies** within agreed timeframes and is subject to increased monitoring.

questions.subject: Economy
questions.questionText:
The Agri-Market Infrastructure Fund (AMIF)
which has been set up for developing and upgrading agricultural marketing infrastructure in the GrAMs and Agriculture Produce Market Committee (APMC), is hosted by:

options.a: National Bank for Agriculture and Rural Development (NABARD)
options.c: National Agricultural Cooperative Marketing Federation of India (NAFED)

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The Government of India has set up the **Agri-Market Infrastructure Fund (AMIF)** with a corpus of Rs. 2000 crore with **National Bank for Agriculture and Rural Development (NABARD)** for development and upgradation of marketing infrastructure in GrAMs and APMCs. **States may access** **AMIF for development and upgradation of marketing infrastructure in rural haats.**
● Development of existing village haats into GrAMs with modern marketing and value addition facilities will **substantially enhance the market accessibility of farmers** including SC/ST and women farmers near to their farm gate and will also facilitate remunerative prices by enabling them to sell their produce directly to consumers and bulk consumers. **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Economy
questions.questionText:
A farmer uses drones to survey crop health and monitor pest outbreaks, while soil sensors automatically trigger irrigation when moisture levels drop. Fertiliser is released only when nutrient levels fall below optimal, and all this data is remotely accessed on a mobile dashboard.
