In case of drought signals, alerts are generated to adjust sowing cycles.
Which of the following emerging technologies best describes the scenario?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Smart farming (Farm automation)** represents the advanced stage of a long history of agricultural mechanization. As per the FAO, mechanization includes all forms of equipment — from simple hand tools to complex motorized machinery — used in farm operations. Traditionally, only the "performing" phase of agriculture (like ploughing, harvesting, spraying) was mechanized. However, modern agricultural automation involves three integrated and cyclic phases:
○ **Diagnosis:** Identifying needs (e.g., water stress, pest presence) through experience or sensor data ○ **Decision-Making**: Determining what action is required and when, based on diagnosis ○ **Performing:** Executing the action through tools, machinery, or fully automated systems
● Advanced systems, such as fruit-harvesting robots, can perform all three phases autonomously, with farmers only overseeing sensor outputs and equipment maintenance. This marks a shift from manual decision-making to sensor-based, automated agriculture, increasing precision and efficiency in farm management.
● The above example illustrates full-cycle farm automation where diagnosis, decisions, and operations are all automated using digital tools.
○ **Diagnosis (Identifying the problem)**: Drones are used to monitor crop health and pest outbreaks and soil sensors detect moisture levels. These tools collect real-time field data. ○ **Decision-Making (What to do and when)**: Based on sensor inputs, systems decide when to irrigate or fertilize. Drought alerts help determine whether to adjust sowing cycles. ○ **Performing (Executing the action)**: Irrigation is triggered automatically when moisture drops. Fertiliser is released only when needed, based on nutrient data. This is automated execution of farm tasks, without manual intervention. ○ **Integration via Dashboard**: All this information is remotely monitored through a mobile dashboard — allowing the farmer to supervise the farm digitally, increasing efficiency and precision. **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Chola and other South Indian Kingdoms and Contact with Southeast Asia
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference to the ‘M-CADWM’ scheme:
I. - It is a sub-scheme of Atal Bhujal Yojana.
II.  	 It aims to develop underground pressurised piped micro-irrigation systems.
III. 	 It transfers the management of irrigation infrastructure from farmers to government departments for better efficiency.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The Union Cabinet recently approved the ‘Modernisation of Command Area Development and Water Management (M-CADWM)’ as a **sub-scheme of the Pradhan Mantri Krishi Sinchayee Yojana** **(PMKSY)**. It is designed to significantly enhance the irrigation infrastructure in India. **So, statement** **I** **is not correct.**
○ The key goal of the M-CADWM is to **modernise the irrigation water supply** **network**, ensuring that irrigation water reaches the designated farming clusters from existing canals or other water sources.
● A key feature of the M-CADWM scheme is the implementation of **underground pressurised, piped** **irrigation systems**, extending up to 1 hectare per farm. This infrastructure will be developed to enhance micro-irrigation practices, enabling farmers to use water more effectively and ultimately increasing agricultural production and productivity. **So, statement** **II is correct.**
● In addition to modernising the irrigation systems, the scheme aims to build sustainable farming practices. It proposes the **Irrigation Management Transfer (IMT) to Water User Societies (WUS)**, empowering these communities to manage irrigation assets independently. **So, statement** **III is not** **correct.**
○ To ensure long-term success, these societies will receive support for the next five years, helping them connect with **Farmer Producer Organisations (FPOs) or Primary Agricultural** **Cooperative Societies (PACS).** This is expected to profitably improve sustainable water management practices**.**
● This will help farmers with small land-holdings, by improving water-use efficiency through the use of advanced technologies such as **SCADA (Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition) and the** **Internet of Things (IoT).** These technologies will assist in better water accounting and management, directly contributing to increased **Water Use Efficiency (WUE)** at the farm level. **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box:** **Pradhan Mantri Krishi Sinchayee Yojana (PMKSY):**
● It was launched during the year 2015-16, as a **centrally sponsored scheme** with an aim to enhance physical access of water on farms and expand cultivable area under assured irrigation, improve on-farm water use efficiency, introduce sustainable water conservation practices, etc.
● PMKSY is an umbrella scheme, consisting, inter alia, of two major components being implemented by the **Ministry of Jal Shakti,** namely, **Accelerated Irrigation** **Benefit Programme (AIBP), and Har Khet Ko Pani (HKKP).** **Atal Bhujal Yojana:**
● **Atal Bhujal Yojana** is a **central sector scheme worth Rs. 6,000 crore** which aims for sustainable management of groundwater with community participation. It envisages people's participation through the formation of **water budgeting**, preparation & implementation of Gram-panchayat-wise water security plans, etc.
● It is being implemented by the **Ministry of Jal Shakti** (earlier known as Ministry of Water Resources, River Development and Ganga Rejuvenation). The scheme is being funded by the **Government of India and the World Bank on a 50:50** **basis**. **Relevance**: The Union Cabinet recently approved the ‘Modernisation of Command Area Development and Water Management (M-CADWM)’ as a sub-scheme of the Pradhan Mantri Krishi Sinchayee Yojana (PMKSY).

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference to organic farming:
I. - Australia is the largest organic producer in the world.
II. 	 In India, Madhya Pradesh is the leading state in terms of area under organic farming.
III. 	 The Participatory Guarantee System in India aims to promote export of highquality organic products based on third-party certification.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Organic farming is a method of farming system which **avoids or largely excludes the use of** **synthetic inputs** (such as fertilizers, pesticides, hormones, feed additives etc) and to the maximum extent feasible rely upon crop rotations, crop residues, animal manures, off-farm organic waste, mineral grade rock additives and biological system of nutrient mobilization and plant protection.
● India is home to **30% of total organic producers** in the world having 2.30 million ha. **Australia is** **the largest organic producer in the world** (35.69 million ha), followed by Argentina and Spain. **India** **ranks fifth** in terms of organic production, which is below Australia. **So, statement I is correct.**
● The leading states in organic cultivation include **Madhya Pradesh at the top** (26 per cent), followed by Maharashtra (22 per cent), Gujarat (15 per cent) and Rajasthan (13 per cent). **So, statement II is** **correct.**
● **PGS-India (Participatory Guarantee System of India)** is a quality assurance initiative that is locally relevant, emphasizes the participation of stakeholders, including producers and consumers and **operates** **outside the frame of third-party certification**. PGS-India is governed by the **Ministry of** **Agriculture and Farmers Welfare** mainly focused for **local / domestic market purposes**. **So,** **statement III is not correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.** **Relevance**: A parliamentary panel has recently proposed the introduction of a Minimum Support Price (MSP) for all organic crops, in addition to the existing practice of declaring MSP for traditional crops.

questions.subject: Economy
questions.questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
Agriculture Market Price/ Cost
Equals to
I. - Procurement price Minimum Support Price
II. - C2 cost Actual cost of production
III. - Central Issue Price Economic cost of production minus subsidy
IV. - Economic Cost of food-grains Minimum Support Price plus the procurement incidentals and distribution cost
How many of the pairs given above are correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Minimum Support Price** (MSP) is the rate at which the government purchases crops from farmers. It is based on a calculation of at least one-and-a-half times the cost of production incurred by the farmers.
○ The National Commission of Farmers (NCF), led by MS Swaminathan, recommended that the MSP should be at least 50 per cent more than the weighted average cost of production. This was also known as the C2+50 percent formula. This includes the input cost of capital and the rent on the land, to give the farmers 50 per cent of the returns. ○ **C2** is the **actual cost of production**, including account rent and interest foregone on land and machinery owned by farmers. According to the commission, the formula to calculate MSP would be MSP= C2+ 50 per cent of C2. **So, pair II is correctly matched.**
● **Procurement price** of a commodity refers to the price at which the government procures the commodity from producers/manufactures for maintaining the buffer stock or the public distribution system.
○ These prices are **announced by the Government of India** **before the harvest season of** **the crop.** ○ At these announced prices, the Government procures the foodgrains (wheat, paddy and coarse grains) in the needed quantity either for maintaining the buffer stock or for the distribution through fair price shops.
○ Procurement prices are fixed generally at a level, which is somewhat higher than the level of minimum support prices but lower than the prevailing market prices. ○ Procurement prices are announced before the sowing season. As a result, the **procurement** **price itself becomes the support price** at which the Government purchased all the foodgrains offered for sale. Procurement prices **also become the MSPs** because the Government was bound to purchase the foodgrains offered by the producers for sale. **So, pair** **I is correctly matched.**
● The **Central Issue Price (CIP)** is the price at which the government makes food grains available to states for distribution under the Public Distribution System (PDS). It is a subsidized price, typically lower than the market price, and the **difference between the CIP and the government's economic** **cost of procuring and distributing the grains is covered by a food subsidy.** The CIP is significantly lower than the market price of food grains. **So, pair III is correctly matched.**
○ For example, under the NFSA, the CIP for rice, wheat, and coarse grains is Rs. 3, Rs. 2, and Rs. 1 per kg, respectively.  If the government's economic cost of producing and distributing a kilogram of rice is Rs. 30, then the subsidy is Rs. 27 per kilogram.
● The **economic cost** of foodgrains procured by the Food Corporation of India (FCI) is of strategic importance, as it influences not only the food subsidy bill but also the country’s **competitiveness in** **international markets for foodgrains**. The FCI reimburses the difference between the economic cost of foodgrains and the issue price.
○ The **economic cost of food grains is a total of Minimum Support Price and bonus** (if any) paid to the farmers plus the **procurement incidentals and distribution cost**. **So, pair** **IV is correctly matched.** ○ FCI's economic cost has three main components - procurement cost, procurement price, and distribution cost.
■ The procurement incidentals are the initial costs incurred during the procurement of
foodgrains. ■ The distribution costs include freight, handling charges, storage charges, losses during
transit and establishment cost. **So, all four of the pairs given above are correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Economy
questions.questionText:
Consider the following: In a _____,  the barriers to entry for the public sector are removed and the private sector is encouraged to enter whereas, in a ______,  public enterprises are made to behave as private enterprises by giving financial autonomy as well as giving autonomy to make investment decisions. Disinvestment is also termed as _____ when a loss-making enterprise is turned over to the private sector because the government can no longer support and sustain it.
Which of the following sets of terms would best fit in the above paragraph?

options.a: Cold Privatisation, Greenfield privatisation, Distressed privatisation
options.b: Greenfield privatisation, Proxy privatisation, Distressed privatisation
options.c: Distressed privatisation, Greenfield privatisation, Proxy privatisation
options.d:
Greenfield privatisation, Distressed
privatisation, Cold privatisation

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Privatisation** is a term that is employed to convey a variety of ideas. The idea that it most prominently suggests is ‘denationalisation’ (in the sense of transferring the ownership of a public enterprise to private hands). Another idea is ‘liberalisation and deregulation’; which unleash forces of competition.
● In terms of policy initiative in the Indian context, **privatisation is generally conceptualized in three** **broad ways**, viz., greenfield privatisation; cold privatisation and disinvestment or divestiture (in particular distressed privatisation).
● Under **greenfield privatisation**, **the barriers to entry including ‘reservation’ for the public** **sector are removed and the private sector is encouraged to enter**. Under this mode actions move on the following lines:
○ removing barriers of entry for the private sector and it is allowed to do economic activity hitherto reserved for public sector, ○ not allowing any new investment or new activities on the part of the public sectors agencies, ○ preferential treatment being given to the private sector for increasing the level of its activities, and ○ in enterprises where private and public sectors have been functioning side-by- side, such as the joint sector, the relative share of the private sector may be increased.
● Under the **Cold Privatisation** or **Proxy Privatisation**, public enterprise made to behave as private enterprises by:
○ **giving financial autonomy** to seek financial assistance directly from the bank/capital market, ○ **giving autonomy to make investment decisions**, ○ entering into a Memorandum of Understanding (MOU) for providing freedom to fix prices, output etc., ○ making subsidies explicit, and ○ taking recourse to corporations, i.e., converting a department enterprise into a corporate entity to ensure distancing.
● **Disinvestment or divestiture** is effected by sale or transfer of shares held by the government directly or through its agencies in enterprises (i.e., public activities organized under enterprise form) to the private sector. When a loss-making enterprise is turned over to the private sector because the government can no longer support and sustain it, this can be termed **‘distressed privatisation.**’ **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Economy
questions.questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
Type of Public Private Partnership Model/ Contract
Share of project cost (Private developer : Government)
I. - Hybrid Annuity Model 60 : 40
II. - Build-OperateTransfer 100 : 0
III. - Engineering, Procurement and Construction
50 : 50
How many of the pairs given above are correctly
matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Public Private Partnership (PPP)** means an arrangement between a government/statutory entity/government owned entity on one side and a private sector entity on the other.
● It is often done for the provision of public assets or public services, through investments being made and/or management being undertaken by the private sector entity, for a specified period of time.
● Several PPP models are employed in India, including: ○ **Build-Operate-Transfer (BOT):** It is a PPP framework where a private entity is granted the rights to **design, finance, construct, and operate** a facility for a fixed duration. **So, pair II** **is correctly matched.**
■ A Build Operate Transfer (BOT) Project is typically used to develop a **discrete asset**
**rather than a whole network** and is generally entirely new or greenfield in nature (although refurbishment may be involved). ■ In a BOT Project the project company or operator **generally obtains its revenues**
**through a fee charged to the utility/ government** rather than tariffs charged to consumers. In common law countries a number of projects are called concessions, such as toll road projects, which are new builds and have a number of similarities to BOTs . ○ **Build-Own-Operate (BOO):** In this arrangement, a private organization takes responsibility for constructing, owning, and operating a facility indefinitely. Unlike BOT, the asset does not revert to public ownership.
■ The government may support the private party with incentives like tax exemptions or
policy support to ensure viability. ○ **BOT-Annuity Model:** Under this framework, the private sector executes the project and receives pre-agreed payments from the government at regular intervals, regardless of revenue generated.
■ These fixed annuity payments are tied to performance and availability metrics, reducing
financial risks for the developer. ○ **Operations & Maintenance (O&M)** / **Service Contracts:** O&M or service contracts are short-term agreements where the government outsources specific tasks, such as operations or maintenance of public assets, to private firms.
■ These contracts do not involve investment in infrastructure creation and are mainly
focused on service delivery. ○ **Engineering, Procurement and Construction (EPC):** It is a contract model where a **private contractor is responsible for all project-related activities, including design,** **procurement of materials, and construction.** **So, pair III is not correctly matched.**
■ The **government finances the project**, while the private firm executes it without
taking long-term operational responsibility. ○ **Hybrid Annuity Model (HAM):** It blends aspects of the EPC and BOT-Annuity formats. In this model, the government covers 40% of the project cost during construction, while the remaining 60% is funded by the private developer. **So, pair I is correctly matched.**
■ Typically, the developer contributes around 20–25% of the cost as equity, with the rest
arranged through debt. ■ The government pays annuities over time, reducing traffic or revenue risks for the private
player. ○ **Design-Build-Finance-Operate (DBFO):** The private partner designs, finances, builds, and operates the project, often under a long-term lease. ○ **Lease-Develop-Operate (LDO):** The private sector leases an existing facility, upgrades it, and operates it for a defined period. **So, only two of the pairs given above are correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: International Relations
questions.microTopic: India and the World (USA, Russia, Middle East, Africa, etc.)
questions.questionText:
With reference to the logistic sector in India,
consider the following statements:
I. - Road transport generates the highest Gross
Value Addition (GVA) within transport
services in India.
II. 	 In rail transport, the growth rate in freight
traffic has been higher than that in passenger
traffic in recent years.
III. 	 India has one of the fastest-growing aviation
industries globally.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Road transport generates the highest Gross Value Addition** (GVA) within transport services. During FY23, road transport accounted for 78 per cent of the total GVA of transport services. Enhancing user convenience on national highways is central to the growth of road transport. In this direction, the government has moved from traditional ways of tolling to digitised tolling by adopting FASTag. In a major step towards road safety, the government has formulated a comprehensive strategy to improve road safety standards on National Highways. **So, statement I is correct.**
● **Indian Railways** (IR) is the fourth largest network in the world. With more people travelling by reserved class and a growth in the transportation of commodities — from coal and iron ore to gunny sacks and rice — the Indian Railways witnessed a year-on-year (y-o-y) surge of over **5 per cent in** **passenger traffic**, and an **increase of 1.68 per cent in freight loading during financial year** **2024-25 (FY25) compared to previous year**, according to the data available with the Ministry of Railways.  **So, statement II is not correct.**
● Recently released industry forecasts show India now handles about 174 million air passenger journeys (2024), making it the world’s third-largest air market. Boeing projects South Asia – driven by India’s booming middle class – will see 8% annual traffic growth over the next 20 years. In practical terms, India’s domestic airlines are adding dozens of new aircraft: CAPA India estimates 1,500-1,700 planes on order for the next few years, expanding the combined fleet of Indian carriers well beyond the current ~800 aircraft. **In short, India is the fastest-growing aviation industry globally, led by robust** **consumer demand and expanding route networks.** **So, statement III is correct.**
○ The growth rate of India’s air passenger traffic appears set to overtake that of China in 2026, and the former is likely to be the world’s fastest-growing large aviation market over the next three decades, per projections from global airports industry body Airports Council International (ACI).
● The **port capacity** expansion has been implemented across all 12 Major Ports. The data in the table indicates a **significant increase in the annual cargo handling capacity at major Indian ports** **from 2014-15 to 2023-24.**
○ The total cargo handled rose from 871.52 million tonnes to 1629.86 million tonnes, marking an
87.01% increase.
○ **Paradip Port experienced the highest growth** at 141.86%, with cargo volumes jumping from 119.8 million tonnes to 289.75 million tonnes. ○ **Kamarajar Port** also saw a notable rise of 154.05%, increasing from 37 million tonnes to 94 million tonnes. ○  Other ports like **Syama Prasad Mookerjee, Chennai, and Deendayal (Gujarat)** also recorded significant growth, highlighting the overall expansion and modernisation of India's port infrastructure during this period. The Union Cabinet has approved the development of a new Major Port at Vadhavan near Dahanu in Maharashtra. **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
With reference to Primary Agricultural Credit
Societies (PACS), consider the following
statements:
I. - They are administered by the State Registrar
of Cooperative Societies (RCS).
II. 	 They are regulated under the Banking
Regulation Act, 1949.
III. 	 They are refinanced by NABARD through
District Central Cooperative Banks (DCCBs)
and State Cooperative Banks (StCBs).
IV. 	 They offer credit for input supply and
marketing of agricultural produce.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Primary Agricultural Credit Societies (PACS)** are grassroots-level cooperative credit institutions registered under the **Cooperative Societies Act of** **the respective states**. They operate at the village level and have individual farmers, artisans, and weaker sections as their member shareholders. PACS represent the **lowest tier of the short-term cooperative** **credit structure**, which also includes District Cooperative Banks (DCCBs) and State Cooperative Banks (StCBs) as higher tiers.
● PACS are governed by Entry 32 of the State List in the Constitution and are **administered by** **the** **respective** **State** **Registrar** **of** **Cooperative Societies (RCS)** as per the provisions of the respective State Cooperative Societies Act.  **So, statement I is correct.**
● They are not regulated by the Reserve Bank of India (RBI) and **are outside the purview of the** **Banking Regulation Act, 1949**. **So, statement II is not correct.**
● PACS are **refinanced by NABARD** through District Central Cooperative Banks (DCCBs) and State Cooperative Banks (StCBs).  **So, statement III is correct.**
● PACS are expected to **mobilize deposits** from their members and combine these funds with borrowings from higher-tier cooperative banks to **provide loans** to needy members. They diversified into credit-linked services such as **input supply**, **storage facilities**, and **marketing** of agricultural produce. **So, statement IV is correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Economy
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference
to the rupee-denominated debt:
I. - Foreign Institutional Investors' (FII)
investment in government treasury-bills is
considered as the rupee-denominated debt.
II. 	 Unlike foreign currency-denominated
external debt, the currency risk is borne by
the borrower in rupee-denominated debt.
Which of the statements given above is/are
correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Rupee-denominated debt** refers to that part of India’s total external debt that is denominated in India’s domestic currency, the Rupee.  In contrast to foreign currency-denominated external debt, the currency risk (the risk arising from appreciation or depreciation of the nominal exchange rate) is **borne** **by the creditor and not by the borrower** in the case of rupee-denominated debt. **So, statement** **II is not correct.**
●   India rupee-denominated debt comprises the following categories: ○ Rupee Debt includes the outstanding defence and civilian state credits extended to India by the erstwhile Union of Soviet Socialist Republics (USSR). The repayment is primarily through exports of goods to Russia. ○ **Rupee-denominated Non-Resident Indian (NRI) Deposits,** including the Non-Resident (External) Rupee Account (NR(E)RA) and Non-Resident Ordinary Rupee (NRO) account. ○ **Foreign Institutional Investors' (FII) investment in Government Treasury-Bills** and dated securities (with such investments subject to a ceiling of US$ 10 billion annually). **So,** **statement I is correct.** ○ **FII’s investment in corporate debt securities** (with such investments subject to a ceiling of US$ 40 billion annually). **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Economy
questions.questionText:
Which of the following statements are correct with reference to currency trade?
I. - Carry trade involves borrowing from a lower interest rate currency to fund the purchase of a higher interest rate currency.
II. 	 Arbitrage is the simultaneous purchase and sale of the same or similar asset in different markets in order to make profit.
III. 	 Currency hedging aims to reduce the effects of currency fluctuations on investment performance.
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Answer: d** **EXplantion:**
● A **currency carry trade is a strategy that involves borrowing from a lower interest rate** **currency to fund the purchase of a higher interest rate currency**. A trader uses this strategy in an attempt to capture the difference between the rates which can be substantial depending on the amount of leverage used. **So, statement I is correct.**
○ Carry trades come with risk because they're often highly leveraged and overcrowded. Carry traders **can recognize profit or loss on the value appreciation or depreciation** of the currency pair in addition to potentially earning interest.
● **Arbitrage** is the simultaneous **purchase and sale of the same or similar asset in different** **markets** **in order to profit** from tiny differences in the asset’s listed price. It exploits short-lived variations in the price of identical or similar financial instruments in different markets or in different forms.  **So, statement II is correct.**
● **Currency hedging** is an attempt to reduce the effects of currency fluctuations on investment performance. It is similar to insurance, which is bought to protect oneself from an unforeseen event. **So, statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: IPR, Institutions and Policies for IPR
questions.questionText:
Which of the following are the objectives of the Food Corporation of India (FCI)?
I. - Effective price support operations for safeguarding the interests of the farmers
II. 	 Distribution of foodgrains throughout the country for public distribution system
III. 	 Maintaining a satisfactory level of buffer stocks of foodgrains
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
The main objectives of food management are procurement of food grains from farmers at remunerative prices, distribution of food grains to consumers, particularly the vulnerable sections of society at affordable prices and maintenance of buffer stock for food security and price stability.
● The **Food Corporation of India (FCI) was setup under the Food Corporation's Act 1964** , in order to fulfill following objectives of the Food Policy:
○ **Effective price support operations** for safeguarding the interests of the farmers. **So, point** **I is correct.** ○ **Distribution of foodgrains throughout the country** for public distribution system. **So,** **point II is correct.** ○ **Maintaining a satisfactory level of operational and buffer stocks of foodgrains** to ensure National Food Security. **So, point III is correct.**
● Since its inception, FCI has played a significant role in India's success in transforming the crisis management oriented food security into a stable security system.
● The **Food Corporation of India (FCI) has to maintain a part of stocks of procured food grains** **in the form of buffer** as a measure of food security to guard against situations of scarcity of food grains and also to enable the Government to intervene effectively and positively to stabilize prices in time of undue increase in the market. **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.** **Relevance**: According to official data, India's rice stocks in government warehouses rose 18% from a year ago to a record high for the start of June 2025.

questions.subject: Economy
questions.questionText: Which one of the following authorities grants the AGMARK certification?

options.b: Food Safety and Standards Authority of India (FSSAI)
options.d: Agricultural and Processed Food Products Export Development Authority (APEDA)

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **AGMARK** is a certification mark employed on agricultural products in India, assuring that they **conform** **to a set of standards** approved by the Directorate of Marketing and Inspection, an agency of the Government of India. The AGMARK is legally enforced in India by the Agricultural Produce (Grading and Marking) Act of 1937 (and amended in 1986).
● **The Directorate of Marketing and Inspection (DMI)**, an attached Office of the Department of Agriculture and Farmers Welfare under Ministry of Agriculture & Farmers Welfare, is the issuing authority of the Licence. AGMARK is a **third-party assurance system** for agricultural products produced and consumed in India.
● Generally, AGMARK certification is voluntary. However, AGMARK certification is **mandatory for** **select agricultural products** in India, including blended edible vegetable oils, fat spreads, ghee, sesame (til) oil, honey, Kangra tea, Carabia callosa, and Honey Dew, as per quality control regulations issued by the Food Safety and Standards Authority of India (FSSAI).
● AGMARK certification is applicable to **both food and non-food agricultural items, including fiber** **crops**. The certified items include:
○ **Non-food agricultural products**: hides, skins, goat hair, wool, animal casings, bristles, lac, tobacco, myrobalans, tendu leaves, senna leaves, sun hemp, jute, cotton, aloe and palmyra fibres — highlighting AGMARK’s extension to raw materials and traditional fibers. ○ **Food commodities**: ghee, vanaspati, honey, tea, spices, cereals, pulses, edible oils, fruits, vegetables, mushrooms, dairy and meat products, sweeteners like gur and sugarcane, and animal feed like tapioca. **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: IPR, Institutions and Policies for IPR
questions.questionText:
Consider the following:
I. - Packaged natural mineral water
II. 	 Milk-powder
III. 	 Switches for domestic and similar purposes
IV. 	 Portland Slag Cement
A mandatory Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS)
Certification is required for how many of the
above products?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS)** is the **national certification body in India** under the umbrella of the **Ministry of Consumer Affairs, Food & Public Distribution, Government of** **India.** On April 1, 1987, it effectively replaced the Indian Standards Institution (ISI) organization and took over their functions.
● The BIS was established under the BIS Act 2016 for the harmonious development of the activities of **standardization, marking and quality certification of goods** and for matters connected therewith or incidental thereto.
● BIS certification refers to the process of **obtaining a quality standard certificate** from the BIS for manufacturing and selling various products in India. The BIS certification scheme is basically **voluntary** in nature.
○ However, for a number of products compliance to Indian Standards is made **compulsory** by the Central Government under various considerations viz. public interest, protection of human, animal or plant health, safety of environment, **prevention of unfair trade practices** and national security. For such products, the Central Government directs **mandatory use of** **Standard Mark** under a Licence or Certificate of Conformity (CoC) from BIS through issuance of Quality Control Orders.
● While **packaged natural mineral water** and **milk-powder** are exempted, **switches for domestic** **and similar purposes** and **portland slag cement** require a mandatory BIS Certification. **So, points** **I and II are not correct and points III and IV are correct.** **So, a mandatory Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS) Certification is required for only two of the** **above products.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.** **Relevance**: The government has recently approved hallmarking standards for 9 carat gold, making it officially part of the Bureau of Indian Standards’ (BIS) hallmarking system.

questions.subject: Economy
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference
to the Price Support Scheme:
I. - It is usually implemented when prices of
notified pulses, oilseeds and copra fall below
the Minimum Support Price (MSP) during
peak harvesting periods.
II. 	 For 2024-25, Central nodal agencies procured
Tur, Urad, and Masur up to 100% of the
State’s production.
Which of the statements given above is/are
correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
The **Price Support Scheme** is implemented by the Government of India under India’s umbrella scheme **PM-** **AASHA** (Pradhan Mantri Annadata Aay Sanrakshan Abhiyan), launched in 2018.
● The **Department of Agriculture & Cooperation** implements this scheme for procurement of oilseeds, pulses, and cotton, through the National Agricultural Cooperative Marketing Federation of India **(NAFED),** which is the Central nodal agency, at the MSP (minimum support price) declared by the government. In addition to the NAFED, the **Food Corporation of India (FCI)** will take up PSS operations in states/districts.
● The PSS is usually implemented when **prices of notified pulses, oilseeds and copra fall below the** **Minimum Support Price (MSP)** during peak harvesting periods, and is intended to ensure farmers receive remunerative prices for their crop. **So, statement I is correct.**
● In order to incentivize the farmers contributing for the enhancement of domestic production of pulses and to reduce the dependence on imports, the Government has approved the procurement of **Tur,** **Urad and Masur** under Price Support Scheme (PSS) equivalent to **100% of the production of the** **state** for the procurement year 2024-25. **So, statement II is correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box:**
● **Pradhan Mantri Annadata Aay SanraksHan Abhiyan (PM AASHA):** It is an umbrella scheme aimed at ensuring **remunerative prices to the farmers** for their produce. PM AASHA has **three components**, and states can choose whichever one they wish to implement.
o PM-AASHA encompasses the **Price Support Scheme (PSS), Price**
**Deficiency Payment Scheme (PDPS), Market Intervention** **Scheme (MIS),** and **Price Stabilisation Fund (PSF).**
● **Price Deficiency Payment Scheme (PDPS):**  Under PDPS, it is proposed to cover **all oilseeds** for which **MSP** is notified. Direct payment of the difference between the MSP and the selling/model price will be made to pre-registered farmers selling his produce in the notified market yard through a transparent auction process.
● **Pilot of Private Procurement and Stockist Schemes (PPPS):** In addition to PDPS, for oilseeds, states have the option to roll out PPSS on a pilot basis in **selected districts/Agricultural Produce Market Committee (APMCs) of** **districts** involving the participation of private stockists.  This scheme will allow private players to participate in the procurement of crops. The pilot district/selected APMC(s) of the district will cover one or more crops of oilseeds for which MSP is notified. **Relevance**: The Central Government has recently approved the purchase of 54,166 tonnes of moong and 50,750 tonnes of groundnut crops under the price support scheme (PSS).

questions.subject: Economy
questions.questionText:
In the context of Indian economy, the Revolving
Fund, Vulnerability Reduction Fund and
Community Investment Fund are associated with:

options.c: Assistance to the Self Help Groups (SHGs)
options.d: Crop insurance to small and marginal farmers

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Revolving Fund** (RF) is a mechanism **to accelerate the process of internal lending and** **increase the size of the corpus in the development of Self Help Groups** (SHGs). The main purpose of providing RF to SHG is to inculcate the habit of thrift and credit among the  Self Help Group members and build their institutional capacities on the management of external funds. This is **a** **permanent fund of the SHG**. It resides with the SHG and is used for interloaning among the members of the SHG. The average size of the Revolving Fund is Rs. 20,000/- to Rs. 30,000/- per  SHG.
● **Vulnerability Reduction Fund (VRF)** is routed to SHGs through federations (primary and secondary level) to support the livelihoods of members and initiate collective activities for the members. It is disbursed against Micro Credit Plans (MCPs) and Vulnerability Reduction plans (VRF) that are developed by the institutions. The institutions fund these MCPs through their resources or RF/CIF or bank loans. VRF is **a fund given to the Village Organisations (VOs) as a Revolving Fund given by the** **Cluster Level Federations.** It is given to address the vulnerabilities like food insecurity, health risk, sudden sickness/hospitalization, natural calamity, etc., faced by the household(s) or community.
● **Community Investment Fund** (CIF) is provided **to develop credit worthiness of the**
**community institutions** among the external formal financial institutions and create an opportunity for
poor women to learn financial management skills. CIF can be used for **socio-economic activities** i.e.
consumption needs, emergency needs, redemption of high-cost outstanding debt, livelihood
enhancement and other priorities like education, health etc. It is **extended to SHG federations which**
**will be used to advance the loans to SHGs** for undertaking socio-economic activities as per micro
– credit /investment plan (MCP). An amount up to Rs. 2.50 lakh per SHG is given directly to the Cluster
Level Federations (Village Organisations, in case CLF is not yet formed).
**Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Economy
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference
to the Modified Interest Subvention Scheme:
I. - Farmers get access to short term credit at
affordable interest rates through Kisan Credit
Card (KCC).
II. 	 Farmers repaying loans promptly are eligible
for a subsidy of up to 25% of the loan amount.
Which of the statements given above is/are
correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The Modified Interest Subvention Scheme (MISS), launched during the financial year 2006- 07, is a **central sector scheme by the Government of India** which is fully funded for providing cheap credit to the farmers engaged in agriculture and allied activities.
● The scheme is implemented and monitored by the **Reserve Bank of India (RBI) and National Bank** **for Agriculture and Rural Development (NABARD).** Through Public Sector Banks, Regional Rural Banks (RRBs), Cooperative Banks and Private Sector Banks operating in rural and semi urban areas, they ensure that the interest subvention benefits reach the farmers.
● Under MISS, farmers get **short-term loans of up to** **₹****3 lakh** through Kisan Credit Card (KCC) at a **subsidised interest rate of 7%,** with 1.5% interest subvention provided to eligible lending institutions. **So, statement I is correct.**
○ The scheme covers short-term crop loans and has been **extended to include working** **capital for activities related to animal** **husbandry and fisheries.** For loans taken exclusively for animal husbandry or fisheries, the interest benefit is applicable up to ₹2 lakh. ○ Through the **Union Budget 2025-26** the government introduced an increase of shortterm crop loan limits under MISS from ₹3 lakh to **₹****5 lakh**.
● Under the scheme, farmers repaying loans promptly are eligible for an **incentive of up to 3% as a** **Prompt Repayment Incentive (PRI),** effectively reducing their interest rate on KCC loans to 4%. **So,** **statement II is not correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.** **Relevance**: The Government recently approved the continuation of the Modified Interest Subvention Scheme (MISS) for 2025-26 under which farmers get short-term credit at an affordable rate through Kisan Credit Card (KCC).

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: IPR, Institutions and Policies for IPR
questions.questionText:
The exports of which one of the following
commodities have been the highest for the FY
2024-25?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Exports of fruits and vegetables (fresh and processed) reached record highs in 2024–25.** This growth was part of a broader surge in India’s agri-exports, which rose 6.4% to $51.9 billion overall.
○ The spike in exports was attributed to increased demand for Indian produce in global markets. ○ However, imports of fruits (notably dry fruits such as almonds, pistachios, walnuts, apples, dates, figs, and raisins) have also risen, indicating both strong domestic demand and production gaps. ○ India remains a net exporter in the fruits and vegetables category, but rising imports may narrow the trade surplus.
● **In FY23, India exported 4.7 mt of wheat, valued at $1.52 billion. However, exports dropped** **significantly to 0.19 mt in FY24** and further to 2,749 tonnes during April-February 2024-25.
● **Cotton exports have collapsed, and India has become a net importer.** Exports of raw cotton reached a high of $4.3 billion in 2011–12 and $3.7 billion in 2012–13, but have sharply declined since then.
○ Declining domestic production output dropped from 398 lakh bales (2013–14) to 291 lakh bales (2024–25) has forced India to import cotton, especially as there have been no significant yield gains post the introduction of GM Bt cotton.
● **India has become a net importer of pepper, despite being a traditional producer.** ○ While **spices as a category (including chilli, cumin, turmeric, etc.) continue to see** **robust export growth**, the situation is different for pepper.
○ The shift is due to **domestic production constraints** and strong domestic demand, alongside the availability of cheaper pepper from other origins. ○ In spices, India is both an exporter and an importer. In 2023-24, **India’s imports of pepper** **(34,028 tonnes)** and cardamom (9,084 tonnes) exceeded its corresponding exports of 17,890 tonnes and 7,449 tonnes. In other words, it was a net importer of these two traditional plantation spices. **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: International Relations
questions.microTopic: India and the World (USA, Russia, Middle East, Africa, etc.)
questions.questionText:
India ranks first globally in the production of
which of the following?
I. - Milk
II. 	 Jute
III. 	 Eggs
IV. 	 Sugar
Select the correct answer using the code given
below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Agriculture, with its allied sectors**, is the largest source of livelihoods in India. 70 percent of its rural households still depend primarily on agriculture for their livelihood, with 82 percent of farmers being small and marginal.
● **Milk:** India is ranked **first in milk production** contributing 24.76% of global milk production. **Uttar** **Pradesh** is the highest milk-producing state in India. **So, point I is correct.**
● **Jute:** **India** is the **world's largest producer of raw jute and jute goods,** contributing to over 50 percent and 40 percent respectively of global production. **West Bengal** alone accounts for over 50 percent raw jute production. **So, point II is correct.**
● **Eggs:** India is the **second largest egg producer** in the world, after China. The top 5 egg producing States are **Andhra Pradesh (17.85%),** Tamil Nadu (15.64%), Telangana (12.87%), West Bengal (11.37%) and Karnataka (6.62%). These states together contribute 64.37% of the total egg production in the country. **So, point III is not correct.**
● **Brazil is the largest producer of sugar followed by India. So, point IV is not correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box:**
● India is the world’s **2****nd** **largest producer of food grains, fruits and vegetables** **and the 2****nd** **largest exporter of sugar.**
● India ranks **5****th** **in meat production** in the world.
● India is the **largest producer (25% of global production), consumer (27% of** **world consumption) and importer (14%) of pulses** in the world.
● India's annual milk production was 165 MT (2017-18), making
● India has the world's **second-largest cattle population**.
● It is the **second-largest producer of rice, wheat, sugarcane, cotton** **and groundnuts, as well as the second-largest fruit and vegetable** **producer,** accounting for 10.9% and 8.6% of the world fruit and vegetable production, respectively.
● India with 109.85 million buffaloes holds the **largest population of buffaloes** in the world. **Relevance**: For the first time since 1919, India will be counting its pastoral livestock, as part of the 21st Livestock Census which was launched recently.

questions.subject: International Relations
questions.microTopic: Places in News and Geopolitical Hotspots (Ukraine, Taiwan Strait, Hormuz, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
**Statement I:** The adoption of e-tractors can significantly reduce the cost of cultivation for Indian farmers in the long run.
**Statement II:** e-tractors are currently covered under PM-DRIVE and receive direct subsidy support.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?

options.a: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct and Statement II explains Statement I
options.b: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct but Statement II does not explain Statement I
options.c: Statement I is correct but Statement II is incorrect
options.d: Statement I is incorrect but Statement II is correct

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Electric tractors (e-tractors)** can meet the needs of Indian farmers and bring significant benefits like lower emissions, reduced diesel costs, and less noise and vibration, which would improve health outcomes for farm workers.
● E-tractors reduce emissions while saving diesel costs for farmers. Because they also produce less noise and vibrations, e-tractors can help **reduce** **hearing** **loss** and **mitigate** **multiple health risks** associated with longterm use of diesel tractors. Therefore, it can significantly reduce the cost of cultivation for Indian farmers in the long run. **So, statement** **I is correct.**
● Unlike their diesel counterparts, electric tractors have fewer moving parts, resulting in fewer replacement parts and reduced wear and tear. The absence of oil changes, fuel filter replacements, and exhaust system upkeep means that farmers experience a significant reduction in **maintenance costs** over the lifetime of electric tractors.
● The **PM E-DRIVE scheme’s** initial announcement mentioned incentives for “other emerging EVs,” but **purchase incentives for e-tractors have not yet been notified** by the Ministry of Heavy Industries. **So, statement II is not correct.** **So, statement I is correct but Statement II is incorrect.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Governor Generals and Their Policies, Administrative Developments, Judicial Developments, Civil Services, Police, Military
questions.questionText:
Which of the following can lead to economies of scale in production processes?
I.  	 Small Farmers, Large Field (SFLF) model
II. 	 Cooperative farming and pooling of resources
III. 	 Mechanization and use of modern technology
IV. 	 Fragmentation of land holdings
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Small Farmers, Large Field (SFLF) model** is founded on the principles of aggregation and **achieving the economics of scale**, through strengthening backward and forward integration along the supply chain and lowering costs by synchronising key agricultural operations from field preparation to harvest. **So, point I is correct.**
○ The SFLF model was **conceptualised in Vietnam in 2011**. The SFLF model **has taken** **different forms in Vietnam.** Some are formal, with **farmers physically pooling their land** **and setting up companies** that operate like private businesses. The shareholders here are farmers themselves. But there are also many informal SFLF entities, wherein **farmers have** **retained their individual holdings and come together only for synchronisation** and harmonisation of select agricultural operations to improve efficiency and lower costs.
● **Cooperative farming** is a system where farmers come together to pool their resources—such as land, labor, machinery, and capital—and work collectively to achieve better productivity and profitability. Each farmer retains ownership of their land but collaborates in farming activities to reduce costs, increase yields, and share profits fairly. By pooling resources, farmers can **reduce production costs** and increase efficiency. **So, point II is correct.**
● The use of modern technology **contributes to the increase in agricultural productivity**. In fact, it is one of the few areas where labour-saving devices have been fully utilized. A farmer can thus do the work of many men and women with the help of machines. This not only saves time but also **reduces** **cost** and increases production. **So, point III is correct.**
● **Land fragmentation reduces yields through changes in marginal outputs of agricultural** **inputs**. Especially in areas with high opportunity costs of labor, the negative impact is more obvious. Thus, moderate expansion of the size of the plot can reduce the average cost, implying that agriculture can achieve economies of scale within each plot. **Economies of scale can be developed by keeping** **farm size constant**, reducing the number of plots, and expanding the size of each plot. **So, point IV** **is not correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: IPR, Institutions and Policies for IPR
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference to the National Livestock Mission:
I. - The beneficiary share of livestock insurance premium for all categories and areas is 50%.
II.	 The State Animal Husbandry Department is the implementation agency for the mission.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
The **Department of Animal Husbandry & Dairying**, Government of India is implementing the scheme of **National Livestock Mission** since the financial year 2014-15.  The mission aims towards **employment** **generation, entrepreneurship development**, **increase in per animal productivity** and thus targeting increased production of meat, goat milk, egg and wool under the umbrella scheme Development Programme. The excess production will help in the **export earnings** after meeting the domestic demands.
● Insurance premium and Subsidy provided under the scheme: ○ The rate of premium should not exceed: ■ 4.5% in Normal Area, 5.5% in NER/ Himalayan States for annual policies, ■ 8% in Normal Area, 9% in NER/ NER/ Himalayan States for two-year policies ■ 11% in Normal Area, 11.5% in NER/ NER/ Himalayan States for three year policies.
● The **beneficiary share of premium for all categories and areas is 15%.**  The remaining share of the premium will be shared by Central Government at the rate of 60:40 (for states other than Himalayan and NER states), 90:10 (for Himalayan / NER states) and 100% (for UTs) basis as per the funding pattern of the Government. **So, statement I is not correct.**
● Implementation agencies for the missions will be the **State Animal Husbandry Department** and State Implementation Agencies. **So, statement II is correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.** **Relevance**: Recently, Telangana was awarded the ‘Best Performing State’ award at the Entrepreneurship Development Conclave 2025 in implementing the National Livestock Mission Entrepreneurship Development Programme.

questions.subject: Economy
questions.questionText: If you are a resident of India who has invested in a company located in the United States (US stocks). When you receive a dividend or any income from these investments, then that will be subject to tax in which country?

options.d: Neither in India nor in USA

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● To ease the income tax burden the Indian government has signed Double Taxation Avoidance Agreements (DTAA) with several countries. In such cases, where India has a DTAA with a country, then Indian investors in that country can get a tax credit for the tax paid abroad and **adjust that against** **the tax that is payable in India.**
● **In the case of the US**, India has already signed the DTAA. Regarding capital gains, the US Tax authorities do not deduct tax on capital gains of US non-residents. Hence, Indians will be exempt from any tax on capital gains in the US. However, **dividends are subject to withholding tax at 25% in** **the US.**
● **If an individual invests in US stocks, the dividend income is first taxed in the USA** at source (withholding tax, usually 25% or lower depending on the India–US DTAA). Since the individual is a resident of India, **his global income is taxable in India as well.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: International Relations
questions.microTopic: Global Groupings and Agreements (FTA, Paris Agreement, NPT, QUAD, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to the Global Forum on Transparency and Exchange of Information for Tax Purposes:
I. - It is operated by OECD.
II. 	 All G20 countries are its members.
III. 	 It implements international standards for closer co-operation between tax authorities.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **The Organization for Economic Co-operation and Development (OECD)** operates the **Global Forum on Transparency and Exchange of Information for Tax Purposes**, which assesses countries on their compliance with international tax transparency and information-sharing standards. **So, statement I is correct.**
● The Global Forum brings 173 countries and jurisdictions together, **including all G20 countries and** **financial centres**. The majority of its members are developing countries. Together they work on an equal footing to put an end to offshore tax evasion. **So, statement II is correct.**
● The Global Forum delivers through: ○ monitoring the **implementation of the standards**. ○ conducting peer reviews to assess the effectiveness of the implementation ○ capacity building and technical assistance to support its members
● The Global Forum is the leading international body working to **secure the effective implementation** **of transparency and exchange of information standards** worldwide and more specifically, the exchange of information on request (EOIR) and automatic exchange of information (AEOI).  **So,** **statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: IPR, Institutions and Policies for IPR
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with
reference to the Generalised Scheme of Preferences + (GSP +):
I. - It  is a trade mechanism developed by the European Union (EU).
II. 	 Middle-income or high-income countries are not eligible for GSP+ benefits.
III. 	 It completely removes all tariffs for all products.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Generalised Scheme of Preferences (GSP+)** is a trade mechanism developed by the European Union (EU) that establishes a unilateral trade relationship between the EU and developing or underdeveloped countries. **So, statement I is correct.**
● The EU’s (GSP+) gives developing countries a special incentive to pursue sustainable development and good governance. Eligible countries have to implement 27 international conventions on:
○ human rights ○ labour rights ○ the environment ○ good governance
● In return, the EU cuts its import duties to zero on more than two thirds of the tariff lines of their exports.
● In order to be eligible for the GSP+ mechanism, a country needs to fulfil the following criteria: ○ The country is a developing country that does not have any other type of special trade agreement with the EU. ○ The **country has not yet achieved the status of middle-income or high-income** **countries** for three years in a row, as defined by the World Bank. **So, statement II is correct.** ○ The country needs to fulfil cumulatively two “vulnerability” criteria: ■ The import-share criterion: the EU imports a low level of products from this country. Its
average import-share over three years is lower than 6.5% ■ The diversification criterion: the country’s exports into the EU are concentrated in a few
products. These products have to find themselves in the seven largest sections of the GSP product list and have to make up for 75% of the country’s export into the EU. ○ The country has to meet the sustainable development criterion: it has to ratify 27 conventions relative to human rights, good governance, and sustainable development, without reservations. Further, the country cannot gravely fail to implement the conventions.
● The **GSP+ mechanism completely removes all tariffs for a little over 66% of all products** **present in the EU’s official product list**. It functions according to the same principles as laid out in the section on Standard GSP. The only difference is that products are not classified according to being sensitive or non-sensitive products. **So, statement III is not correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.questionText:
Which one of the following statements is **not**
correct with reference to the coal sector in India?

options.a:
India is the second largest consumer of coal
in the world.
options.b:
Coal contributes less than half to the national
energy mix.
options.c:
India's coal dispatch has crossed the one
billion tonnes for the first time in 2024-25.
options.d:
Mission Coking Coal has reduced import
dependency for coal.

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● With the **fifth-largest coal reserves and as the second-largest consumer in the world**, coal remains crucial for India. **China is the world's largest consumer of coal**, accounting for more than half of global demand. **So, option (a) is correct.**
● Coal is vital to India’s energy needs, supplying over half of the country’s power. Despite renewable energy growth, coal-based thermal power will remain essential, with its share projected at 55% by 2030 and 27% by 2047. **It contributes 55% to the national energy mix and fuels over 74% of total power** **generation.** **So, option (b) is not correct.**
● India’s coal production has reached 1047.57 million tonnes (MT) in FY 2024-25, compared to 997.83 MT in FY 2023-24, marking a 4.99% growth. Coal dispatch has also crossed the One BT milestone, with total dispatch reaching 1024.99 MT (Provisional) in FY 2024-25, up 5.34% from 973.01 MT in FY 2023-24.
○ **Coal dispatch refers to the process of transporting and distributing that coal to** **various consumers**, including power plants and industrial facilities.  **So, option (c) is correct.**
● Coal imports fell 8.4% to 183.42 MT in April-December 2024 from 200.19 MT in the same period of FY 2023-24, saving $5.43 billion (₹42,315.7 crore) in foreign exchange. **Government initiatives like** **Commercial Coal Mining and Mission Coking Coal boosted domestic coal output by 6.11%** **during this period, reducing import dependence.**  **So, option (d) is correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the answer.**

questions.subject: Economy
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference
to the Participatory Guarantee Scheme (PGS):
I. - The ‘PGS-Green’ certification applies to
agricultural products that are completely free
of chemicals.
II. 	 The ‘PGS-Organic’ certification is granted
to those agricultural products that are fully
certified organic.
Which of the statements given above is/are
correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Organic agriculture** is a whole system of farming that restores, maintains and enhances the ecological balance.
● **Participatory Guarantee Scheme (PGS)** is a process of **certifying organic products**, which ensures that their production takes place in accordance with laid-down quality standards. The certification is in the form of **a** **documented logo or a statement**.
● PGS is a quality assurance initiative that is **locally relevant, emphasizes the participation of** **stakeholders,** including producers and consumers, and which operates **outside the framework of** **third-party certification.**
● PGS India programme provides two types of certifications: ○ **PGS-Green Certificate:** It is granted to agricultural products that are **undergoing** **conversion to organic practices.** These products originate from farms that have started transitioning from conventional to organic methods **but have not yet completed** the required conversion period. As a result, they **cannot be marketed as “organic”;** however, they may carry the **PGS-Green logo** along with the label **“Under Conversion to Organic”. So,** **statement I is not correct.**
■ The maximum duration for holding PGS-Green status is **up to three years for annual**
**crops and up to four years for plantation or perennial crops**. After this designated period, if the farm does not meet organic certification standards, it must either qualify for full PGS-Organic status or exit the certification system.
● **PGS-Organic Certificate:** It is granted to those agricultural products that are **fully certified organic** under the PGS and can be marketed as **'organic' with the PGS-India Organic logo. So, statement** **II is correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
I. - International Year of
Cooperatives
II. - International Year of Millets
III. - International Year of Soil
IV. - International Year of Pulses
How many of the pairs given above are correctly
matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The year **2025** was declared as the **International Year of Cooperatives by the UN General** **Assembly**. It was declared under the theme “Cooperatives Build a Better World”, recognizing their role in driving sustainable development and advancing the UN SDGs. **So, pair I is not correctly** **matched.**
○ To mark the year, COPAC and the ICA have launched an updated “Building a Better World Together” SDG brief series, offering fresh insights, global case studies, and policy recommendations that highlight the unique contributions of cooperatives to sustainable development and inclusive growth.
● The **International Year of Millets (2023)** was proposed by the Government of India and received endorsements from various FAO bodies and the UN General Assembly in March 2021. The main aim was to **raise awareness and direct policy attention to the nutritional and health benefits of** **millets**, promote their cultivation under adverse and changing climatic conditions, and encourage increased investment in millet R&D and extension services. **So, pair II is correctly matched.**
● The **68th UN General Assembly** declared **2015 as the International Year of Soils.** The Food and Agriculture Organization (FAO) was nominated to implement IYS 2015 in collaboration with governments and the United Nations Convention to Combat Desertification. The main aim was to raise awareness and understanding of the importance of soil for food security and ecosystem functions. **So,** **pair III is not correctly matched.**
● **The International Year of Pulses was celebrated in 2016**, as declared by the UN General Assembly. The Food and Agriculture Organization (**FAO**) was responsible for implementing activities throughout the year. The main objective was to raise awareness about the nutritional benefits of pulses and highlight their important role in sustainable food production and food security. **So, pair IV is not** **correctly matched.** **So, only one of the pairs given above is correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Economy
questions.questionText:
Consider the following:
I. - Increasing the Repo Rate
II. 	 Selling US dollars in the forex market
III. 	 Increasing customs duty
IV. 	 Easing restrictions on External Commercial
Borrowings (ECBs)
V. 	 Encouraging Foreign Portfolio Investment
(FPI)
How many of the above measures are most likely
to be undertaken to control the depreciation of the
Indian Rupee?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Rupee Depreciation** refers to a **fall in the value of the Indian Rupee (INR) relative to a foreign** **currency**, usually the **US Dollar (USD)**, in the **foreign exchange market.** To prevent rupee depreciation, the Reserve Bank of India (RBI) and the Government of India employ various **monetary and capital account** **measures** aimed at increasing foreign currency inflows and reducing demand for foreign exchange.
● When the RBI raises the **Repo Rate**, interest rates in the economy rise. This makes Indian debt instruments like government bonds more attractive to foreign investors. **Higher returns** draw in more foreign capital, increasing the **supply of US dollars** in the Indian market, which helps support the rupee. **So, point I is correct.**
● The RBI intervenes in the currency market by **selling US dollars** from its forex reserves when the rupee falls sharply. By doing so, it **increases the supply of dollars** and prevents further depreciation. This helps maintain **exchange rate stability**. **So, point II is correct.**
● Raising import duties might reduce the import bill and improve the trade balance slowly, but it **does not** **bring in foreign capital or immediately support the rupee**. It is not a standard tool to control currency depreciation. **So, point III is not correct.**
● When restrictions on External Commercial Borrowings (ECBs) are relaxed, Indian companies can borrow more from foreign lenders. This **brings in US dollars** as loans are disbursed, adding to India's forex reserves and **easing pressure on the rupee. So, point IV is correct.**
● If the government makes it easier or more profitable for foreign investors to invest in Indian stocks or bonds by reducing taxes or simplifying rules then **more dollars come in** through FPI. This **improves** **dollar supply**, strengthening the rupee. **So, point V is correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to
the e-commerce in India:
I. - Foreign Direct Investment (FDI) up to 49%
is allowed in the inventory-based models.
II. 	 Marketplace platforms are not allowed to sell
products from companies in which they have
equity.
Which of the statements given above is/are
correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**E-commerce** in India operates under two primary models: inventory-based and marketplace-based. An ecommerce entity refers to a company, including foreign-owned or controlled ones, engaged in e-commerce operations under the Companies Act, 1956 or 2013  or FEMA, 1999 provisions. In the **inventory-based** **model**, the e-commerce entity owns the goods and sells directly to consumers, while in the **marketplace** **model**, it merely facilitates transactions between buyers and sellers through a digital platform.
● Foreign Direct Investment (FDI) up to 49% is permitted in the inventory-based e-commerce model **only** **under strict conditions: the platform must sell ‘Made in India’ products,** be founded and managed by Indian citizens, and raise funds domestically. This regulation prevents foreign companies from fully owning inventory-based platforms but allows partial ownership to encourage Indian entrepreneurship and investment. **So, statement I is correct.**
● The marketplace model allows 100% FDI because the platform acts only as an intermediary without owning the inventory. However, the platform is **prohibited from influencing the pricing or holding** **inventory of the sellers**. This ensures that the e-commerce company functions purely as a facilitator, maintaining a clear distinction from the inventory model.
● **Marketplace platforms are not allowed to sell products from companies in which they have** **equity.** Additionally, any single seller cannot contribute more than 25% of the platform’s total turnover. This rule prevents conflicts of interest and market dominance by any one seller, ensuring fair competition and transparency on the platform. In fact, e-commerce marketplaces are strictly prohibited from selling goods from any company in which they or their group entities hold equity participation. **So, statement** **II is correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** The Union Ministry of Consumer Affairs recently issued notices to few e-commerce models for deploying “dark patterns”—UI tactics meant to mislead consumers.

questions.subject: Economy
questions.questionText:
Consider the following information with
reference to the types of industries in India:
Industry
Investment in Plant and Machinery or Equipment
Turnover
I. - Micro Up to ₹2.5 crore Up to ₹10 crore
II. - Small Up to ₹25 crore Up to ₹100 crore
III. - Medium Up to ₹100 crore Up to ₹1000 crore
In how many of the above rows is the given
information correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
The **Micro, Small, and Medium Enterprises (MSME)** sector is a vital pillar of India's economy, contributing significantly to employment generation, industrial output, and exports. Recognizing the evolving scale of businesses, the **Government of India revised the MSME classification in the Union Budget 2025,** **effective from April 1, 2025**. This revised classification **increased the investment limit by 2.5 times** **and doubled the turnover limit** to better support scaling businesses and make them eligible for various government incentives such as priority lending, tax benefits, and market access.
● As per the Union Budget 2025 revision, a **Micro Enterprise** is defined as one with an **investment up** **to** **₹****2.5 crore** and a **turnover up to** **₹****10 crore**. This is a significant increase from the previous ₹1 crore and ₹5 crore limits. The objective is to help micro units scale gradually without the fear of immediate upward reclassification. **So, row I is correctly matched.**
● A **Small Enterprise** under the new criteria is allowed to invest up to **₹****25 crore** in plant and machinery and can have an annual **turnover of up to** **₹****100 crore**. This enhanced threshold provides room for micro enterprises to grow into the small category without losing MSME benefits. **So, row II is correctly** **matched.**
● According to the **2025 revised criteria**, a **Medium Enterprise** can invest up to **₹****125 crore** (not ₹100 crore) and have a **turnover up to** **₹****500 crore** (not ₹1000 crore). **So, row III is not correctly** **matched.** **So, in only two of the above rows the given information is correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Economy
questions.questionText:
With reference to the electricity sector in India,
consider the following statements:
I. - Generation, Transmission and Distribution
of power are delicensed activities and open
to the private sector.
II.	 UDAY is an optional debt restructuring
scheme for DISCOMs of States to improve
their operational efficiency.
III.	 A Renewable Purchase Obligation (RPO)
mandates the DISCOMs to procure a certain
percentage of their electricity from renewable
energy sources.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **The electricity sector in India** requires significant investments in creating the necessary infrastructure such as **generating stations, transmission and distribution lines**. It also requires significant planning to ensure adequate capacity building in advance to cater to demand. Unlike most other goods, electricity cannot be stored at scale. This requires supply and demand to be matched at all instances. For regulation, the electricity sector has been demarcated into three sub-sectors, i.e., generation, transmission, and distribution.
● **Generation** is the process of producing power using various sources of energy.  **Transmission** refers to carrying high voltage power from generation plants to distribution sub-stations through a transmission grid. **Distribution** refers to transferring electricity from sub-stations to individual consumers via a distribution network.
● **Generation was delicensed and opened to private investment in 1991.** **Transmission and** **Distribution are licensed activities as per the Electricity Act, 2003.  So, statement I is not** **correct.**
● In 2015, the Government of India (Ministry of Power) launched **UDAY Scheme to aid operational** **and financial turnaround of Power Distribution Companies (DISCOMs) owned by any state.** UDAY is basically a debt restructuring plan for DISCOMs to improve their operational efficiency. It was kept optional for states. **So, statement II is correct.**
○ Joining states to undertake 75% debts of their respective DISCOMs, while the remaining 25% debts will be issued to DISCOMs in the form of bonds. ○ These states will receive additional priority funding under numerous schemes such as Deen Dayal Upadhyaya Gram Jyoti Yojana (DDUGJY) and Integrated Power Development Scheme (IPDS). ○ This scheme was established with a vision to provide affordable and accessible 24×7 power to all. ○ It also aims to provide a solution for revenue-side efficiency and cost-side efficiency and envisages reform measures in the following sectors – generation, transmission, distribution, coal and energy efficiency**.**
● **A Renewable Purchase Obligation (RPO) i**s a regulatory mechanism in India that mandates electricity distribution companies (DISCOMs) to procure a **certain percentage of their electricity** **from renewable energy sources.** This policy aims to promote the adoption of renewable energy and reduce reliance on fossil fuels. RPOs are defined under the Electricity Act of 2003 and the National Tariff Policy. **So, statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Governor Generals and Their Policies, Administrative Developments, Judicial Developments, Civil Services, Police, Military
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
**Statement I:**
Brownfield projects can help to deal with the
increasing demand for modern infrastructure in
densely populated cities.
**Statement II:**
Brownfield projects work on underutilised or
abandoned sites in urban areas, increasing their
economic value.
**Statement III:**
The per unit cost of land acquisition for Brownfield
projects is generally less than for the Greenfield
projects.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of
the above statements?

options.a:
Both Statement II and Statement III are
correct and both of them explain Statement I
options.b:
Both Statement I and Statement II are correct
and Statement II explains Statement I
options.c:
Only one of the Statements II and III is
correct and that explains Statement I
options.d:
Neither Statement II nor Statement III is
correct

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Greenfield and brownfield projects** refer to two different approaches in development, particularly in urban planning, construction, and software development. Greenfield projects involve building on undeveloped land, while brownfield projects involve redeveloping or renovating existing sites. For example, the widening/development of existing roads is categorized as Brownfield, whereas construction of a project on a new alignment is categorized as a Greenfield project.
● Redevelopment of brownfield sites is a **strategic response to the demand for modern** **infrastructure** in densely populated cities. Such projects can revitalise neighbourhoods, improve urban aesthetics, and create economic value. **So, statement I is correct.**
