**Relevance:** India’s household savings declined for the third consecutive year, falling to 18.1% of GDP in FY24.

questions.subject: Economy
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference
to the crawling peg currency exchange rate system:
I. - It is used to control currency rate when there
is a threat of currency devaluation.
II. 	 It is often established to provide a degree of
currency exchange stability between trading
partners.
Which of the statements given above is/are
correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● A **crawling peg** is a system of exchange rate adjustments in which **a currency with a fixed exchange** **rate is allowed to fluctuate within a band of rates.** The par value of the stated currency and the band of rates may also be adjusted frequently, particularly in times of high exchange rate volatility.
○ Countries operating under a pegged exchange rate regime “peg” their currency’s value to a foreign currency or some unit of account (e.g., gold, the European currency unit, etc.).
● Crawling pegs are **often used to control currency moves when there is a threat of devaluation** due to factors such as inflation or economic instability. **So, statement I is correct.**
● The primary objective when a crawling peg is established is to **provide a degree of stability between** **trading partners**, which may include the controlled devaluation of the pegged currency to avoid economic upheaval. Due to **high inflation rates and fragile economic conditions**, the currencies of Latin American countries are commonly pegged to the U.S. dollar. As a pegged currency weakens, both the **par value and the bracketed range can be adjusted incrementally to smooth the** **decline** and maintain a level of exchange rate predictability between trading partners. **So, statement** **II is correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Economy
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
I. - Increase in the Foreign Institutional
Investments (FII) can lead to the appreciation
of the domestic currency.
II. 	 Increase in the Foreign Direct Investment
(FDI) inflow leads to the depreciation of the
real exchange rate.
Which of the statements given above is/are
correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● A **Foreign Institutional Investor (FII)** is an investor or investment fund investing in a country outside of the one in which it is registered or headquartered.
● Foreign Institutional Investments (FII) involve large capital flows into or out of a country. When FIIs invest heavily, it increases the demand for the domestic currency, leading to appreciation. Conversely, **sudden withdrawals can create depreciation pressure, destabilizing exchange rates and** **impacting economic stability**. **So, statement I is correct.**
● **Foreign Direct Investment (FDI)  inflow leads to a real exchange rate appreciation**, that is, FDI has a direct impact on the exchange rate. FIIs also have an impact on exchange rate and FIIs investment in India can lead to rupee appreciation and **disinvestment can** lead to its depreciation. FIIs inflows lead to the appreciation of the currency of the host countries. Accordingly, FIIs investment in India can lead to the appreciation of the Indian rupee with several other currencies and vice versa. **So,** **statement II is not correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Economy
questions.questionText:
Which of the following statements are correct with
regard to the Purchasing Power Parity (PPP)?
I. - It refers to the same cost of goods in two
countries when measured in the same
currency.
II. 	 If purchasing power parity is equal to one,
then the goods will be available at the same
rate in both markets.
Which of the statements given above is/are
correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The price of one currency in terms of the other is known as the exchange rate. If the real exchange rate of the Indian Rupee with respect to the United States Dollar is equal to one, then both the currencies are at purchasing power parity. This means that **goods cost the same in two countries when** **measured in the same currency. So, statement I is correct.**
○ For instance, if a pen costs $4 in the US and the nominal exchange rate is Rs 50 per US dollar, then with a real exchange rate of 1, it should cost Rs 200 (50 × 4) in India.
● **If purchasing power parity is equal to one, then the goods will be available at the same rate** **in both markets.** So, it would **neither be cheaper or expensive for the Indians to import goods** from the United States. If the real exchange rises above one, this means that goods abroad have become more expensive than goods at home. **So, statement II is correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Interior of Earth and Continental Theories
questions.questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
Trade policy Feature
I. - Comparative advantage A country produces good at a lower opportunity cost
II. - Absolute advantage A produces more goods than other producers with the same quantity of resources
III. - Specialization A country allocates most of its resources towards the production of a particular good
How many of the pairs given above are correctly
matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Comparative advantage** describes a situation in which an **individual, business or country can** **produce a good or service at a lower opportunity cost than another producer**. **So, pair I is** **correctly matched.**
○ For example, because it has an abundance of maple trees, Canada can produce maple syrup at a very low opportunity cost in relation to avocados, a fruit for which its climate is less suited. ○ Mexico, on the other hand, with its ample sunshine and warm climate. can grow avocados at a much lower opportunity cost in terms of maple syrup given up than Canada.
● **Absolute advantage** describes a situation in which an individual, business or country can **produce** **more of a good or service than any other producer** with the same quantity of resources. **So, pair** **II is correctly matched.**
● **Specialization** refers to a situation when **a country allocates most or all of its resources** towards the production of a particular good or service. **So, pair III is correctly matched.** **So, all three of the pairs given above are correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer**.

questions.subject: Economy
questions.microTopic: International Organisations (WTO, IMF, World Bank, RCEP, FTAs, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Consider the following:
I. - Special Drawing Rights (SDRs) of the
Government of India
II. 	 Gold held by the RBI
III. 	 Foreign Currency Assets of the RBI
IV.	 Reserve Tranche Position (RTP) in the
International Monetary Fund (IMF)
How many of the above are the components of
forex reserves of India?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **foreign exchange reserves,** also called forex reserves, are the reserves maintained by the Reserve Bank of India (RBI) to provide import cover, protect against external shocks, make international payments and hedge against exchange rate risks.
● The RBI has the primary responsibility of collection, compilation and dissemination of data relating to foreign exchange reserves.
● The four components of forex reserves of India are **Foreign Currency Assets** of the RBI, **Gold** held by the RBI, **Special Drawing Rights (SDRs)** of the Government of India and the **Reserve Tranche** **Position (RTP)** in the International Monetary Fund (IMF). **So, points I, II, III and IV are correct.** **So, all four of the above are the components of forex reserves of India.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
**Foreign Currency Assets (FCAs):**
● These assets are **held in diverse currencies** like US Dollar, Euro, Pound Sterling, Australian Dollar, etc. An appreciation in the value of the US dollar against major international currencies translates into lower US dollar equivalent for assets held in other currencies.
● The FCAs also include the effect of appreciation or depreciation of non-US units like the euro, pound and yen held in the foreign exchange reserves. **Special Drawing Rights (SDRs):**
● The IMF created the SDR as a supplementary international reserve asset in **1969,** when currencies were tied to the price of gold and the US dollar was the leading international reserve asset.
● The SDR serves as the **unit of account of the IMF** and other international organisations.
● The SDR is an international reserve asset. The SDR is **not a currency,** but its value is based on a **basket of five currencies** - the US dollar, the euro, the Chinese renminbi, the Japanese yen and the British pound sterling.
● **Individuals and private entities cannot hold SDRs.** IMF members – and the IMF itself – hold SDRs and the IMF has the authority to approve other holders, such as Central Banks and multilateral development banks.
● The SDR value in terms of the US dollar is **determined daily based on the spot** **exchange rates** observed at around noon London time.
● The IMF **reviews the SDR basket every five years,** or earlier if warranted, to ensure that it reflects the relative importance of currencies in the world’s trading and financial systems.

questions.subject: Economy
questions.questionText:
Consider the following:
I. - Maharashtra
II. 	 Karnataka
III. 	 Tamil Nadu
IV. 	 Gujarat
How many of the above States contribute more than ten percent to the Gross State Domestic Product (GSDP) for the FY 2024-25?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● India, the **world’s fifth-largest economy**, with several regions emerging as major economic powerhouses, driving growth through high Gross State Domestic Product (GSDP) and per capita income.
● The projected **Gross State Domestic Product (GSDP) figures for FY 2024-25** highlight the economic distribution across key Indian states. **Maharashtra** continues to dominate with the highest projected GSDP at ₹42.67 lakh crore, **contributing 13.3% to the national GDP,** reflecting its strong industrial, financial and services base. **So, point I is correct.**
● **Tamil Nadu** follows with ₹31.55 lakh crore, accounting for **8.9% of national GDP,** supported by a robust manufacturing and services sector, while also recording one of the highest per capita incomes at ₹3.50 lakh (2023-24). **So, point III is not correct.**
● **Karnataka and Gujarat**, with projected GSDPs of ₹28.09 lakh crore and ₹27.9 lakh crore respectively, **contribute over 8% each,** benefiting from diversified sectors such as IT, manufacturing and trade. **So,** **points II and IV are not correct.**
● Uttar Pradesh, despite having a high GSDP projection of ₹24.99 lakh crore and the largest population, shows a low per capita income of ₹0.96 lakh, indicating wide income distribution challenges. West Bengal, with a GSDP of ₹18.8 lakh crore and **5.6% contribution to the national GDP,** has a moderate per capita income of ₹1.57 lakh, reflecting its mixed performance in agriculture, industry and services. **So, only one of the above states contributes more than ten percent to the Gross State Domestic** **Product (GSDP) for the FY 2024-25.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.questionText:
India’s demographic dividend, which began in the early 1980s and is expected to last until 2040. It is considered a “golden period” for which of the following reasons?
I. - Working-age population rises
II. 	 The dependent population decreases
III. 	 The youth get better education
IV.  	The youth mainly engage in non-farm jobs
Select the correct answer using the code given below :
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Demographic dividend is a golden period** as the economic growth potential results from a high proportion of working-age people relative to dependents (children and the elderly). This "golden period" is a **window of** **opportunity for rapid economic development**.
● **India’s demographic dividend** began in the early 1980s and will end in 2040.
● During this time, the share of the **working-age population is rising**, and that of the **dependent** **population falling**, which would mean an attendant economic growth if the right economic policies create enough non-farm jobs. **So points I and II are correct.**
● The demographic dividend is a golden period when the **young are getting better educated** and desperately need non-farm jobs. **So, points III and IV are correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
**Key Facts About Second Demographic Dividend:**
● The second demographic dividend emerges after the first dividend fades, when fertility rates decline and the population ages.
● Economic and financial mechanisms can encourage workers to accumulate assets such as **property, pensions, and personal savings**, leading to financial independence in old age.
● If these mechanisms are well-developed, population aging contributes positively by promoting **sustainable family and government support systems**, higher capital formation, greater productivity, and rising per capita income.
● The second demographic dividend can be **long-lasting and even larger** than the first, provided the right institutions and policies are in place.
● The second demographic dividend operates in **two major ways**: ○ **Greater Accumulation of Wealth**: Lower fertility and longer life expectancy encourage people to save more during their working years.
■ Wealth peaks by old age, allowing individuals to remain self-sufficient
rather than dependent on families or the state. ○ **Greater Investment in Human Capital**: Rising incomes and smaller family sizes enable parents to invest more in the **health and education** **of children**. With fewer children to support, each child receives greater resources, thereby raising productivity in the long run.

questions.questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
Type of Economy Example Sector
l. Yellow Economy Health and social care
II. - White Economy Desert tourism
III. - Orange Economy Entertainment industry
IV. Gold Economy Digital finance
How many of the pairs given above are correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Colour-coded ‘economies’** label **thematic economic sectors**—each emphasises distinct resources, activities, and policies (e.g., Blue for aquatic resources, Green for sustainability, Gold for tech). **They guide targeted strategies** for growth, resilience, and inclusive development and jobs.
● **There are several colours of the economy — these include:** ○ **Blue Economy:** sustainable marine and freshwater management, including fisheries, aquaculture, and water-based tourism, ensuring ecosystem health and economic viability. ○ **Yellow Economy:** new development models and opportunities in the **desert**, such as **desert** **tourism**, use of renewable energies, sustainable agriculture and enterprise development. **So,** **pair l is not correctly matched.** ○ **White Economy: health and social care sectors**, critical for societal well-being and in facing challenges like resilience in a poly-crisis context. **So, pair ll is not correctly matched.** ○ **Orange Economy:** cultural and creative industries, encompassing arts, **entertainment**, fashion, design, architecture, advertising, software, publishing, and research and development. **So,** **pair lll is correctly matched.** ○ **Gold Economy:** technology and digitalization, activities leading high-tech innovation such as **digital finance**, advanced technologies, and premium digital services. **So, pair lV is correctly** **matched.** ○ **Green Economy:** with a focus on the Circular Economy: sustainability, renewable energy, green manufacturing, and eco-friendly practices aimed at minimizing environmental footprints and optimizing the use of resources. ○ **Silver Economy:** targets the aging population’s needs and aspirations, focusing on entrepreneurship, older workers, retirement planning, and age-friendly products and services. **So, only two of the pairs given above are correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Economy
questions.questionText:
If a Manufacturing unit is set up in any Special Economic Zone (SEZ) in India, then which of the following benefits will be accrued to such a unit?
I. - It will receive 100% income tax exemption on export income for the first 5 years of its operation
II.	 It  will not require any approvals from the State Governments
III.	 It will not require any license for import of goods
IV.	 It will be given a duty free import/domestic procurement of goods
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Special Economic Zones (SEZ)** have been created in India with a view to attract large foreign investments and create employment opportunities. SEZ is a specifically delineated duty-free enclave and is deemed to be foreign territory for the purposes of trade operations and duties and tariffs. They have been established to promote manufacturing and exports from India.
● The salient features of the SEZ scheme are:○ A designated duty free enclave to be treated as a territory outside the customs territory of India for the purpose of authorised operations in the SEZ; ○ **No licence required for import; So, point III is correct.** ○ Manufacturing or service activities allowed; ○ The Units are only required to achieve Positive Net Foreign Exchange to be calculated cumulatively for a period of five years from the commencement of production;
○ Domestic sales subject to full customs duty and import policy in force; ○ Full freedom for subcontracting; ○ No routine examination by customs authorities of export/import cargo; ○ SEZ Developers /Co-Developers and Units enjoy Direct Tax and Indirect Tax benefits as prescribed in the SEZs Act, 2005.
● The incentives and facilities offered to the units in SEZs for attracting investments into the SEZs, including foreign investment include:
○ **Duty free import/domestic procurement of goods for development, operation and** **maintenance of SEZ units.** ○ **100% Income Tax exemption on export income for SEZ** units under Section 10AA of the Income Tax Act **for first 5 years, 50% for next 5 years thereafter and 50% of the** **ploughed back export profit for next 5 years.** **So, point I is correct.** ○ Exemption from Central Sales Tax, Exemption from Service Tax and Exemption from State sales tax. These have now subsumed into GST and supplies to SEZs are zero rated under IGST Act, 2017. ○ Other levies as imposed by the respective State Governments. ○ **Single window clearance for Central and State level approvals. So, point II is not** **correct.** ○ Supplies to SEZ are zero rated under IGST Act, 2017. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: IPR, Institutions and Policies for IPR
questions.questionText:
In the context of Indian economy, consider the following industries:
I. 	 Electronic Aerospace
II. 	 Defense equipment
III. 	Tobacco products
Which of the above require a mandatory license?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Industrial licensing in India is mandated by the Industries (Development and Regulation) Act passed in 1951. Industrial Licenses are granted to applicants for the manufacture of items that are compulsorily licensable under the Industries (Development and Regulation) Act, 1951 (IDR Act). The Licenses are **granted by the Department for Promotion of Industry and Internal Trade (DPIIT), Ministry** **of Commerce and Industry.**
● Industrial License is essentially required for all the existing and new industrial units and industrial units that are being expanded.
● Only four industries have been retained under compulsory licensing under the Industries (Development & Regulation) Act, 1951:
○ **Electronic Aerospace and Defence Equipment** ○ **Industrial Explosives** ○ Hazardous Chemicals ○ **Cigars and cigarettes of tobacco** and manufactured tobacco substitutes. **So, points I, II and** **III are correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**
